ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-1
Chapter 2: Program History
2.1 Overview
Continuous measurement has long been viewed as a possible alternative method for collecting
detailed information on the characteristics of population and housing; however, it was not
considered a practical alternative to the decennial census long form until the early 1990s. At that
time, demands for current, nationally consistent data from a wide variety of users led federal
government policymakers to consider the feasibility of collecting social, economic, and housing
data continuously throughout the decade. The benefits of providing current data, along with the
anticipated decennial census benefits in cost savings, planning, improved census coverage, and
more efficient operations, led the Census Bureau to plan the implementation of continuous
measurement, later called the American Community Survey (ACS). After years of testing,
outreach to stakeholders, and an ongoing process of interaction with key data users—especially
among expert statisticians and demographers—the Census Bureau expanded the ACS to the
planned full sample size for housing units (HUs) and the household population in 2005 and
added the group quarters (GQs) population in 2006.
The history of the ACS can be divided into five distinct stages: design, development,
demonstration, full implementation, and continuous improvement. The Census Bureau first
proposed the concept of continuous measurement in the 1990s. From 1990 to 1993, design
proposals were drafted and vetted. In the development stage (1994 through 1999), the Census
Bureau tested early prototypes of continuous measurement for a small number of sites. During
the demonstration stage (2000 to 2004), the Census Bureau carried out large-scale, nationwide
surveys and produced reports for the nation, the states, and large geographic areas. The full
implementation stage began in January 2005, with an annual HU sample of approximately 3
million addresses throughout the United States and 36,000 addresses in Puerto Rico. In 2006,
approximately 20,000 group quarters and the population within them were added to the ACS so
that the data fully describe the characteristics of the population residing in geographic areas.
Since the full implementation stage began in 2005 and 2006, we have been continuously
improving. The continuous improvement stage has included a fundamental reexamination of the
systems and processes that underlie the ACS and an exploration of new methods, techniques, and
approaches designed to improve the ACS program and the Census Bureau’s relationships with
stakeholders and data users.
2.2 Design Origins and Early Proposals
In 1981, Leslie Kish introduced the concept of a rolling sample design in the context of the
decennial census (Kish, 1981). While Kish was conducting his research, the Census Bureau also
recognized the need for more frequently updated data. In 1985, Congress authorized a mid-
decade census, but funds were not appropriated. In the early 1990s, Congress expressed renewed

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-2
interest in an alternative to the once-a-decade census. Based on Kish’s research, the Census
Bureau began developing continuous measurement methods in the mid-1990s.
The Census Bureau developed a research proposal for continuous measurement as an alternative
to the collection of detailed decennial census sample data (Alexander, 1993g), and Charles
(Chip) Alexander, Jr. developed three prototypes for continuous measurement (Alexander,
1993i). Based on staff assessments of operational and technical feasibility, policy issues, cost,
and benefits (Alexander, 1994e), the Census Bureau selected one prototype for further
development. Designers made several decisions during prototype development. They knew that
if the survey were to be cost-efficient, the Census Bureau would need to collect data through a
self-response mail questionnaire. They also determined that a nonresponse follow-up would be
conducted to reduce nonresponse bias. Like the decennial census, the survey would be
mandatory. It was decided that the survey would use both telephone and personal visit
nonresponse follow-up methods. In addition, the designers made critical decisions regarding the
prototype’s key definitions and concepts (such as the residence rule), geographic makeup,
sampling rates, and use of population controls.
With the objective of producing 5-year cumulations for small areas at the same level of sampling
reliability as the long-form census sample, a monthly sample size of 500,000 HUs was initially
suggested (Alexander, 1993i), but this sample size drove costs into an unacceptable range. When
potential improvements in nonsampling error were considered, it was determined that a monthly
sample size of 250,000 would generate an acceptable level of reliability.
2.3 Development
Development began with the establishment of a permanent Continuous Measurement Staff in
1994. This staff continued the development of the survey prototype and identified several design
elements that proved to be the foundation of the ACS:
• Data would be collected continuously by using independent monthly samples.
• Three modes of data collection would be used: mail, telephone nonresponse follow-
up, and personal visit nonresponse follow-up.2F
3
• The survey reference date for establishing housing unit occupancy status, and for
many characteristics, would be the date the data were collected. Certain data items
would refer to a longer reference period (for example, “last week” or “past 12
months”) anchored by the response date.
3 The ACS introduced internet as a self-response mode in 2013. A centralized telephone nonresponse follow-up
was discontinued in October 2017, although field representatives who conduct personal visit nonresponse follow-up
may also call respondents and respondents may call the telephone questionnaire assistance line.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-3
• The survey’s estimates would be controlled to population and (beginning in 2000)
housing estimates from the Population Estimates Program.
• All estimates would be produced by aggregating data collected in the monthly
surveys over a year so that they would be reported annually based on the calendar
year.
The documentation of early development took several forms. Beginning in 1993, a group of 20
reports, known as the Continuous Measurement Series (Alexander, 1992; 1993a−1993i; 1994a−
1994f; and 1995a−1995b; Alexander and Wetrogan, 1994; Cresce, 1993), documented the
research that led to the final prototype design. Plans for continuous measurement were
introduced formally at the American Statistical Association’s (ASA) Joint Statistical Meetings in
1995. Love et al. (1995) outlined the assumptions for a successful survey, while Dawson et al.
(1995) reported on early feasibility studies of collecting survey information by telephone.
Possible modifications of continuous measurement data also were discussed (Weidman et al.,
1995).
Operational testing of the ACS began in November 1995 at four test sites: Rockland County,
NY; Brevard County, FL; Multnomah County, OR; and Fulton County, PA. Testing was
expanded in November 1996 to encompass areas with a variety of geographic and demographic
characteristics, including Harris County, TX; Fort Bend County, TX; Douglas County, NE;
Franklin County, OH; and Otero County, NM. This testing was undertaken to validate methods
and procedures and to develop cost models for future implementation; it resulted in revisions to
the prototype design and identified additional areas for research. Further research took place in
numerous areas, including small-area estimation (Chand and Alexander, 1996), estimation
methods (Alexander et al., 1997), nonresponse follow-up (Salvo and Lobo, 1997), weighting in
ACS tests (Dahl, 1998), item nonresponse (Tersine, 1998), response rates (Love and Diffendal,
1998), and the quality of rural data (Kalton et al., 1998).
Operational testing continued, and in 1998 three counties were added: Kershaw County, SC;
Richland County, SC; and Broward County, FL. The two counties in South Carolina were
included to produce data to compare with the 1998 Census Dress Rehearsal results, and Broward
County was substituted for Brevard County. In 1999, testing expanded to 36 counties in 26 states
(U.S. Census Bureau, 2004e). The sites were selected to represent different combinations of
county population size, difficulty of enumeration, and 1990−1995 population growth. The
selection incorporated a variety of geographic areas as well as areas representing different
characteristics, such as racial and ethnic backgrounds, migrant or seasonal populations,
American Indian reservations, changing economic conditions, and predominant occupation or
industry types. Additionally, the Census Bureau selected sites with active data users who could
participate in evaluating and improving the ACS program. Based on the results of the operational
tests, revisions were made to the prototype and additional areas for research were identified.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-4
Tests of methods for the enumeration of people living in GQs were also held in 1999 and 2001.
These tests focused on the methodology for visiting GQs, selecting resident samples, and con-
ducting interviews. The tests selected GQ facilities in all 36 test counties and used the procedures
developed in the prototyping stage. The results of the tests led to the modification of sampling
techniques and revisions to data collection methods.
While the main objective of the development phase testing was to determine the viability of the
methodologies used, it also generated usable data. Data tables and profiles were produced and
released in 1999, providing data on demographic, social, economic, and housing topics.
Additionally, public use microdata sample (PUMS) files were generated for a limited number of
locations during the period of 1996 through 1998. PUMS files show data for a sample of all
HUs, with information on the housing and population characteristics of each selected unit. All
identifying information is removed, and other disclosure avoidance techniques are used to ensure
confidentiality.
2.4 Demonstration
In 2000, a large-scale demonstration was undertaken to assure Congress and other data users that
the ACS was capable of producing the demographic, social, economic, and housing data
previously obtained from the decennial census long-form sample.
During the demonstration stage, the ACS was called the Census 2000 Supplementary Survey
(C2SS). Its primary goal was to provide critical assessments of feasibility, quality, and
comparability with the 2000 Census to demonstrate the Census Bureau’s ability to implement the
ACS fully. Although ACS methods had been successful at the test sites, it was vital to
demonstrate national implementation. Additional goals included refining procedures, improving
the understanding of the cost structure, improving cost projections, exploring data quality issues,
and assuring users of the reliability and usefulness of ACS data.
The C2SS was conducted in 1,239 counties, of which 36 were the ACS test counties from 1999,
and 1,203 were new to the survey. It is important to note that only the 36 ACS test counties used
the proposed ACS sample design. The others used a state-based primary sampling unit stratified
design similar to the Current Population Survey (CPS). The sample of counties was designed to
produce estimates for all states and for geographic areas of 250,000 or more. The annual sample
size increased from 165,000 HUs in 1999 to 866,000 HUs in 2000. The test sites remained in the
sample throughout the C2SS, and through 2004 were generally sampled at higher rates than the
C2SS counties. The sampling rates for 1999-2001 were higher than the full implementation
design so that the 3-year estimates for this period from the ACS in these counties were
comparable to the planned 5-year period estimates of a fully implemented ACS, as well as to
data from the 2000 Census.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-5
Eleven reports issued during the demonstration stage analyzed various aspects of the program.
There were two types of reports: methodology and data quality/comparability. The methodology
reports reviewed the operational feasibility of the ACS. The data quality and comparability
reports compared C2SS data with the data from the 2000 Census at the state and national level as
well as comparisons of ACS 3-year data to the 2000 Census data for the ACS test sites.
Report 1 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2001) found that the C2SS was operationally successful, its
planned tasks were completed on time and within budget, and the data collected met basic
Census Bureau quality standards. However, the report also noted that certain areas needed
improvement. Specifically, because they coincided with the decennial census, telephone
questionnaire assistance (TQA) and failed-edit follow-up (FEFU) operations were not staffed
sufficiently to handle the large workload increase. The evaluation noted that the ACS would
improve planning for the 2010 Census and simplify its design and that implementing the ACS,
supported by an accurate Master Address File (MAF) and Topologically Integrated Geographic
Encoding and Referencing (TIGER) database, promised to improve the decennial census
coverage. Report 6 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2004c) was a follow-up evaluation of the feasibility of
utilizing data from 2001 and 2002. The evaluation concluded that the ACS was well-managed,
was achieving the desired response rates, and had functional quality control procedures.
Report 2 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2002) concluded that the ACS would provide a reasonable
alternative to the decennial census long-form sample and added that the timeliness of the data
gave it advantages over the long form. This evaluation concluded that, while ACS methodology
was sound, its improvement needed to be an ongoing activity.
A series of reports compared national, state, and limited substate 1-year period estimates from
the C2SS and the 2000 Census. Reports 4 and 10 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2004a; 2004g) noted
differences. For example, although 63 of the 86 housing characteristic estimates differed
significantly between the C2SS and the 2000 Census long-form results at the 90 percent
confidence level, only 23 of these differences were greater than 0.5 percentage points, and only 8
differed by more than 1.0 percentage points. At the national level, distributions of the general
demographic and housing characteristics from the C2SS were very similar to those produced
from the 2000 Census counts. A review of select county-level statistics confirmed most national
findings but showed some count-level differences. The overall conclusion of the C2SS and the
2000 Census comparisons was that the research supported the proposal to move forward with
plans for the ACS.
Report 5 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2004b) analyzed economic characteristics and concluded that
estimates from the ACS and the 2000 Census long form were essentially the same. Report 9
(U.S. Census Bureau, 2004f) compared social characteristics and noted that estimates from both
methods were consistent, with the exceptions of disability and ancestry. The report suggested the
completion of further research on these and other issues.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-6
A set of multiyear period estimates (1999–2001) from the ACS test sites was created to help
demonstrate the usability and reliability of ACS estimates at the county and census tract
geographic levels. Results can be found in Reports 7 and 8 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2004d; 2004e).
These comparisons with the 2000 Census sample data further confirmed the comparability of the
ACS and the 2000 Census long-form estimates and identified potential areas of research, such as
variance reduction in subcounty estimates.
At the request of Congress, a voluntary response test also was conducted during the
demonstration phase. The test, conducted between March and June of 2003, was designed to
examine the impact that a methodological change from mandatory to voluntary response would
have on mail response, survey quality, and costs. Reports 3 and 11 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2003;
2004h) examined the results. These reports identified the major impacts of instituting voluntary
methods, including reductions in self-response rates by about a third (with a self-response rate in
low-response areas of 27.7 percent), reductions in the reliability of estimates, and cost increases
of more than $59 million (in 2003 dollars) annually.
2.5 Full Implementation
In 2003, with the full implementation of the ACS approaching, the American Community Survey
Office (ACSO) came under the direction of the Associate Director for the Decennial Census.
While the Census Bureau had planned to implement the ACS fully in 2003, budget restrictions
pushed back full HU implementation of the ACS and the Puerto Rico Community Survey
(PRCS) to January 2005. The GQ component of the ACS was implemented fully in January
2006.
With full implementation, the ACS expanded to all 3,141 counties in the 50 states and the
District of Columbia and all 78 municipios in Puerto Rico. The annual ACS sample increased
from 800,000 addresses in the demonstration phase to 3 million in full implementation.
Workloads for all ACS operations more than tripled. Monthly mailouts from the National
Processing Center (NPC) went from approximately 67,000 to 250,000 addresses per month.
Telephone nonresponse follow-up workloads, conducted from three telephone call centers,
expanded from 25,000 calls per month to approximately 85,000. More than 3,500 field
representatives (FRs) across the country conducted follow-up visits at 40,000 addresses a month,
up from 1,200 FRs conducting follow-ups at 11,000 addresses each month in 2004. And,
approximately 36,000 addresses in Puerto Rico were sampled every year, using the same three
modes of data collection as the ACS. Beginning in 2006, the ACS sampled 2.5 percent of the
population living in GQs. This included approximately 20,000 GQ facilities and 195,000 people
in GQs in the United States and Puerto Rico.
With full implementation beginning in 2005, population and housing profiles for 2005 first
became available in the summer of 2006 and have been available every year thereafter for
specific geographic areas with populations of 65,000 or more. Three-year period estimates,

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-7
reflecting combined data from the 2005−2007 ACS, were available for the first time late in 2008
for geographic areas with populations of 20,000 or more. Five-year period estimates, reflecting
combined data from the 2005−2009 ACS, became available late in 2010 for areas down to block
groups, census tracts, and small local governments.3F
4 Beginning in 2010, the nation had a 5-year
period estimate, available as an alternative to the decennial census long-form sample, for nearly
all geographic areas recognized by the Census Bureau, including census tracts and block groups.
2.6 Continuous Improvement
With the publication of the first 5-year estimates, the Census Bureau met its goal of replacing the
decennial census long form with the ACS because those estimates were designed to be
comparable to the long form estimates produced following each decennial census. Since then, the
ACS program has been in a continuous improvement stage, which began with a program review.
Program improvements have continued through consultation with external experts and research
and implementation of updates.
The ACS Program Review
The publication of the first 5-year estimates was followed by planning for a detailed review of
the systems and processes that underlie the ACS program. An initial goal of this review, which
began in 2011, was to identify possible opportunities for improvements. By 2012, the review was
well underway and had expanded to include other aspects of the ACS program. In 2013, as part
of what was by then called the ACS Program Review, managers in several divisions that
contributed to the ACS participated in a series of meetings and off-site events designed to
envision the ACS program of the future (U.S. Census Bureau, 2015). Other developments
associated with the program review include:
• The organization of a set of review teams (the highest level of which was comprised
of division chiefs) to function as a set of program management boards. These
boards, including Change Control boards and a Portfolio Management Governing
Board, provide oversight to the review of technical decisions for the program.
• The incorporation of a risk review and formal work request process to acknowledge
the total resources required to implement planned improvements to the program.
• The use of off-site events and organizational management techniques to solicit staff
views more effectively in the day-to-day management of the program.
4 Beginning in 2014, the 3-year estimates were discontinued; however, a small set of 1-year Supplemental
Estimate Tables were added for areas with populations of 20,000 or more. The 2005-2007, 2006-2008, 2007-2009,
2008-2010, 2009-2011, 2010-2012 and 2011-2013 ACS 3-year estimates will remain available to data users, but no
new 3-year estimates will be produced.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-8
• The adoption of more effective documentation processes.
• The recognition of the need for greater involvement with corporate infrastructure
solutions such as adaptive design.
• The creation of an ACS Data Users Group to solicit information more effectively on
what stakeholders need to use ACS estimates and what usability issues arise among
stakeholders with similar interests in ACS data applications.
ACS Stakeholders and External Engagement
The ACS program depends heavily on engaging stakeholders in the development of the program
and seeking stakeholder input as much as possible in decisions affecting ACS data products.
Consultations with stakeholders began early in the ACS development process with the goals of
gaining feedback on the overall approach and identifying potential pitfalls and obstacles.
As a formal ACS testing period was under development, ACS managers started forming plans to
ensure local communities were aware that they were included as test sites for the ACS. ACS
testing was launched in four sites in 1995, as described earlier in this chapter. From March 1996
to November 1999, 31 town hall-style meetings were held throughout the country, with more
than 600 community representatives attending the meetings. Similar meetings took place in the
years to follow. A series of three regional outreach meetings in Dallas, TX; Grand Rapids, MI;
and Seattle, WA, were held in mid-2004, with an overall attendance of more than 200 individuals
representing data users, academicians, the media, and local governments. Other early stakeholder
engagement efforts included the development of special-purpose advisory panels in partnership
with the Committee on National Statistics of the National Academies of Science and a Rural
Data Users Conference held in May 1998 in Alexandria, Virginia, to discuss issues of concern to
representatives of small areas and populations. Annual meetings of individual State Data Center
representatives and affiliate organizations have frequently featured presentations to update
members on the latest ACS program developments and data products.
Changes based on stakeholder input were important in shaping the design and development of
the ACS and continue to influence its form as the ACS program moves forward. A “Symposium
on the ACS: Data Collectors and Disseminators” took place in September 2000. It focused on the
data uses and needs of the private sector. A periodic newsletter, the ACS Alert, was established
to share program information and solicit feedback.4F
5 The Interagency Committee for the ACS
was formed in 2000 to discuss the content and methods of the ACS and how the survey meets the
needs of federal agencies. From 2003 to 2005, the Census Bureau invited federal agencies to
participate in an ACS Federal Agency Information Program designed to arrange meetings at
5 This newsletter no longer exists. The ACS program currently sends monthly emails to subscribers on topics such
as changes to ACS methodology, data releases, and data products.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-9
federal agencies where specific questions by federal agency representatives on the ACS design,
methods, and data products could be addressed by Census Bureau technical experts.
The Census Bureau has also consulted with experts convened by the Committee on National
Statistics (CNSTAT). For example, in 2007, the Committee on National Statistics (CNSTAT)
issued an important report, “Using the American Community Survey: Benefits and Challenges,”
which reflected the input of many stakeholders and addressed the interpretation of ACS data by a
wide variety of users. In 2013, the Census Bureau requested that CNSTAT convene a workshop
on the benefits of the ACS to a broad array of non-federal data users. A summary of the
workshop is described in the CNSTAT publication, “Benefits, Burdens, and Prospects of the
American Community Survey: Summary of a Workshop.” Additional reports published by
CNSTAT are included in the references (see National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and
Medicine 2016 and 2019 and National Research Council 2001, 2007, 2012, 2013, and 2015).
Meetings with the Census Advisory Committees—Bureau of the Census Scientific Advisory
Committee (CSAC) and the Census Bureau National Advisory Committee (NAC) on Racial,
Ethnic, and Other Populations—provide opportunities for ACS staff to receive specific advice
and recommendations on the ACS design, survey methods, and data products. The Census
Bureau’s Field Division and regional directors have played a prominent role in communicating
the importance of participating in ACS data collection to state and local government
representatives and circulating pamphlets and similar publications to explain the ACS program
and its benefits to communities.5F
6 Further, in 2021, the 2020 Census National Partnership
Program transitioned to the Office of Strategic Alliances (OSA). The OSA will continue
leveraging hundreds of national partners obtained during the 2020 Census to help with enterprise
mission work, such as the ACS. This includes working with partners to help program
areas expand ways to disseminate data to current and non-traditional data users and collaborate
to increase responses to the Census Bureau’s economic and demographic censuses and surveys.
The OSA connects program areas with diverse and influential organizations to improve data
collection, data products, and data dissemination.
The ACS program leadership regularly briefs several oversight groups, including the Office of
Management and Budget (OMB), the Government Accountability Office (GAO), and the
Inspector General of the U.S. Department of Commerce (DOC). The Census Bureau also briefs
Members of Congress regularly on multiple aspects of the ACS.
The number and scope of groups and organizations representing ACS stakeholders has expanded
dramatically since the survey was implemented. Some of these organizations represent broad
areas of interest, such as statistical methodology, public opinion research, demography, regional
science, sociology, and geography. Others advocate for specific interests, such as housing,
6 An example of such a publication is the ACS Information Guide.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-10
transportation, and education; some represent population groups, such as older people, veterans,
and American Indians and Alaska Natives; and some are professional groups representing a
specific occupation, such as librarians.
In partnership with the U.S. Census Bureau, the Population Reference Bureau (PRB) and Sabre
Systems created an ACS Data Users Group in the fall of 2012. The purpose of the ACS Data
Users Group is to improve understanding of the value and utility of ACS data and to promote
information sharing among data users about key ACS data issues and applications. The group is
led by a Steering Committee comprised of selected external stakeholders representing a broad
spectrum of data users with different interests. To facilitate communication among ACS data
users, an online community forum and a biennial ACS Data Users Conference were also
established. The inaugural ACS Data Users Conference was held on May 29-30, 2014, in
Washington, DC.
In addition, efforts have been made to promote the international sharing of the Census Bureau’s
experiences with the development and implementation of the ACS. Presentations have been
given to many international visitors who have come to the Census Bureau to learn about surveys
and censuses. Presentations about the ACS have also been made at many international
conferences’ working sessions and meetings. Outreach to stakeholders has been a key
component of launching and gaining support for the ACS program, and its importance and
prominence continue.
Transformation: Expanded Use of Administrative Data
Title 13 of the U.S. Code, Section 6 instructs the Census Bureau to maximize the use of already
available data whenever possible instead of collecting data directly from respondents.
Administrative data are microdata records collected and maintained by administrative (i.e.,
program) agencies and commercial entities. Government and commercial entities maintain these
files to administer programs and provide services. The ACS program has made it a priority to use
alternative data sources and expand the use of administrative data to meet customer needs and
reduce the dependency on traditional methods of data collection (U.S. Census Bureau, 2021,
2022; Thieme, 2022).
The ACS program has long used administrative data to support the operational administration of
the survey, such as frame updates from the U.S. Postal Service and phone numbers for
respondents from commercial entities. More recently, the ACS has moved beyond operational
uses of administrative data and began investigating the use of administrative data to produce
high-quality data products while reducing respondent burden. Once data sources have been
identified, ACS staff determine how to use the data to produce the required data products. See
U.S. Census Bureau, 2018, for the set of guiding principles ACS staff follow to evaluate data
sources.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 2: Program History
2-11
The expanded use of administrative data in the ACS is expected to reduce data collection costs
and improve operational efficiency, reduce respondent burden, and improve the quality of ACS
data products. The long-term goal is to provide data users with information on ACS topics
relying on an active library of data from administrative data, third-party data, and a full suite of
Census Bureau-collected data rather than solely on data collected directly from the public
through the ACS.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-1
Chapter 3: Frame Development
3.1 Overview
The Master Address File (MAF) is the Census Bureau’s official inventory of known housing
units (HUs), group quarters (GQs), and selected non-residential units (public, private, and
commercial) in the United States and Puerto Rico. It serves as the source of addresses for the
American Community Survey (ACS), other Census Bureau demographic surveys, and the
decennial census. It contains mailing and location address information, geocodes, and other
attribute information about each living quarter. A geocoded address is one for which state,
county, census tract, and block have been identified.
The MAF is linked to the Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing
(TIGER) system. TIGER is a database containing a digital representation of all census-required
map features and related attributes. It is a resource for producing maps, tabulating data, and
assigning addresses to geographic locations in geocoding. The resulting database is called the
MAF/TIGER database (MTdb).
The initial MAF was created for the 2000 Census using multiple sources, including the 1990
Address Control File, the U.S. Postal Service’s (USPS’s) Delivery Sequence File (DSF), field
listing operations, and addresses supplied by local governments through partnership programs.
The MAF was used as the initial frame for the ACS, in its state of existence at the conclusion of
the 2000 Census. Updates from frame building and enumeration operations of the 2010 Census
and the 2020 Census were incorporated into the MTdb and included in the ACS sampling frame
beginning in the middle of 2021. The Census Bureau continues to update the MAF using the
DSF and various automated, clerical, and field operations.
The remainder of this chapter provides detailed information on the development of the 2021
ACS sampling frame. Section 3.2 provides basic information about the MAF and its contents.
Sections 3.3 and 3.4 describe the MAF development and update activities for HUs in the United
States and Puerto Rico. Section 3.5 describes the MAF development and update activities for
GQs. Finally, Section 3.6 describes the ACS extracts from the MAF.
3.2 Master Address File Content
The MAF is the Census Bureau’s official inventory of known HUs, GQs, and selected non-
residential units in the United States and Puerto Rico. Each MAF record contains some or all of
the following information: geographic codes, a mailing and/or location address, the physical
characteristics and/or location description of the unit or any relationships to other units,
residential or commercial status, latitude and longitude coordinates, and source and history
information indicating the operation(s) that added or updated the record (see Section 3.3). ACS

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-2
obtains this information from the MAF in files called MAF extracts (see Section 3.6) and uses it
for sampling, data collection, and data tabulation activities.
The geographic codes in the MTdb identify a variety of areas, including states, counties, county
subdivisions, places, American Indian areas, Alaska Native areas, Hawaiian Homelands, census
tracts, block groups, and blocks. Two important geographic code sets are the decennial census
tabulation geography set, based on the January 1 legal boundaries of the most recent decennial
census year, and the current geography set, based on the January 1 legal boundaries of the most
recent year (for example, MAF extracts received in July 2018 reflect legal boundaries as of
January 1, 2018). Each MAF record contains geographic codes from the TIGER database.
Because each record contains a variety of geographic codes, it is possible to sort MAF records
according to different geographic hierarchies. ACS operations generally require sorting by state,
county, census tract, and block.
The MAF contains both city-style and non-city-style mailing addresses. A city-style address is
one that uses a structure number and street name format; for example, 201 Main Street,
Anytown, ST 99988. Additionally, city-style addresses usually appear in a numeric sequence
along a street and frequently follow parity conventions, such as all odd numbers occurring on
one side of the street and even numbers on the other side. They often contain information used to
uniquely identify individual units in multiple-unit structures, such as apartment buildings or
rooming houses. These are known as unit designators and are part of the mailing address.
A non-city-style mailing address is one that uses a rural route (RR) and box number format or a
post office (PO) box format. Examples of these types of addresses are RR 2, Box 9999,
Anytown, ST 99988 and PO Box 123, Anytown, ST 99988.
In the United States, city-style addresses are most prevalent in urban and suburban areas and
accounted for 98.2 percent of all residential addresses in the MAF at the conclusion of the 2010
Census. Following the 2020 Census, this percentage has continued to increase. Most city-style
addresses represent both the mailing and location addresses of the unit. City-style addresses are
not always mailing addresses, however. Some residents at city-style addresses receive their mail
at those addresses, while others use non-city-style addresses (U.S. Census Bureau, 2000b). For
example, a resident could have a location address of 77 West St. and a mailing address of P.O.
Box 123. In other cases, city-style “E-911” addresses have been established so that state
emergency service providers can find a house even though mail is delivered to a rural route and
box number.
Non-city-style mailing addresses are prevalent in rural areas. Because these addresses do not
provide specific information about the location of a unit, finding a rural route and box number
address in the field can be difficult. Post Office Box addresses cannot be located in the field
because they are associated with a post office location, not a structure location.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-3
To help field staff locate non-city-style addresses in the field, the MAF often contains a location
description of the unit and/or its latitude and longitude coordinates.6F
7 The presence of this
information in the MAF makes field follow-up operations possible.
Both city-style and non-city-style addresses can be either residential or non-residential. A
residential address represents a living quarter in which a person or persons live or could live. A
non-residential address represents a structure, or a unit within a structure, that is used for a
purpose other than residence. While the MAF includes many non-residential addresses, it is not a
comprehensive source of such addresses (U.S. Census Bureau, 2000b).
The MAF also contains some address records that are classified as incomplete because they lack
a complete city-style or non-city-style mailing address. Records in this category often just
describe the unit’s location and, usually, its latitude and longitude.
For more information on the MAF, including a description of its content and structure, see U.S.
Census Bureau (2000b).
3.3 Master Address File Development and Updating for the U.S. Housing
Unit Inventory
MAF Development in the United States
For the 1990 and earlier decennial censuses, the Census Bureau compiled address lists from
several sources (commercial vendors, field listings, and others). Before 1990, these lists were not
maintained or updated after a census was completed. Following the 1990 Census, the Census
Bureau decided to develop and maintain a master address list to support the decennial census and
other Census Bureau survey programs to avoid the need to rebuild the address list before each
census. The Census Bureau created the MAF by merging city-style addresses from the:
• 1990 Address Control File7F
8
• Field listing operations8F
9
• USPS Delivery Sequence File (DSF)
7 For example, “E side of St. Hwy, white house with green trim, garage on left side.”
8 The Address Control File is the residential address list used in the 1990 Census to label questionnaires, control
the mail response check-in operation, and determine the response follow-up workload (U.S. Census Bureau 2000a,
p. XVII-1).
9 In areas where addresses were predominantly non-city-style, the Census Bureau created address lists through a
door-to-door canvassing operation (U.S. Census Bureau 2000a, p. VI-2).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-4
• Addresses supplied by local governments through partnership programs, such as the
Local Update of Census Addresses (LUCA)9F
10
• Other 2000 Census activities, including the Be Counted Campaign10F
11
At the conclusion of the 2000 Census, the MAF contained a complete inventory of known HUs
nationwide.11F
12
MAF Improvement Activities and Operations
MAF maintenance is an ongoing and complex task. New HUs are built continually, older units
are demolished, and the institution of addressing schemes to allow emergency response
personnel to find HUs with non-city-style mailing addresses render many older addresses
obsolete. Maintenance of the MAF occurs through a coordinated combination of automated,
clerical, and field operations designed to improve existing MAF records and keep up with the
nation’s changing housing stock and associated addresses. With the completion of the 2000
Census, the Census Bureau implemented several short-term and one-time operations to improve
the quality of the MAF. These operations included Count Question Resolution (CQR),
MAF/TIGER reconciliation, and address corrections from rural directories. For the most part, the
Census Bureau implemented these operations to improve the addresses recognized in the 2000
Census and their associated characteristics. CQR was implemented again after the 2010 Census. The 2010 Census operations improved the coverage and quality of the MAF. The operations
included several nationwide field canvassing and enumeration operations. In preparation for the
2010 Census, the Census Bureau implemented a nationwide address canvassing field operation
(except for remote areas in Alaska and rural Maine) to update the housing unit inventory in the
MAF. Other field operations to support the 2010 Census enumeration identified HU and GQ
corrections, additions, and deletions and updated the MAF with those results. Additionally, the
Census Bureau repeated the same partnership and count coverage programs used for the 2000
Census for the 2010 Census, including the LUCA and the Be Counted programs.12F
13 The Census
10 The 1999 phase of the LUCA program occurred from early March through mid-May 1999 and involved
thousands of local and tribal governments that reviewed more than 10 million addresses.

count coverage programs used for the 2000
Census for the 2010 Census, including the LUCA and the Be Counted programs.12F
13 The Census
10 The 1999 phase of the LUCA program occurred from early March through mid-May 1999 and involved
thousands of local and tribal governments that reviewed more than 10 million addresses. The program was intended
to cover more than 85 percent of the living quarter addresses in the United States in advance of the 2000 Census. The Census Bureau validated the results of the local or tribal changes by rechecking the 2000 Census address list for
all blocks in which the participating governments questioned the number of living quarter addresses. 11 The Be Counted program provided a means to include in the 2000 Census those people who may not have
received a Census questionnaire or believed they were not included on one. The program also provided an
opportunity for people who had no usual address on Census Day to be counted. The Be Counted forms were
available in English, Spanish, Chinese, Korean, Tagalog, and Vietnamese. For more information, see Carter (2001). 12 For details on the address list development for the 2000 Census, see U.S. Census Bureau (2000a). 13 The Census Bureau redesigned the LUCA program for the 2010 Census, allowing participants a choice of
several methods of reviewing the census address or housing unit inventories in their jurisdictions. Participant
feedback was included in the Address Canvassing field operation for verification.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-5
Bureau determined the final 2010 Census status of each HU record in the MAF in late 2010.
These operations improved the MAF extracts used for the ACS sample selection. ACS and the
2010 Census planners worked together closely to assess the impact of the decennial census
operations on the ACS. For details on the 2010 Census operations, see U.S. Census Bureau
(2011).
The 2020 Census used a similar set of frame-building and enumeration operations as previous
censuses. Results from 2020 Census operations began updating the MAF with the start of LUCA
in 2018. The Census Bureau redesigned the Address Canvassing operation to include an in-office
verification of the address list using imagery and administrative records to identify blocks where
costly fieldwork was unnecessary. As in 2010, the 2020 Census frame-building and enumeration
operations improved the address list by identifying additions, deletions, and corrections. The
final 2020 Census inventory of HUs became the main component of the ACS HU frame once the
final 2020 Census results were incorporated into the MAF for ACS data year 2022. For details
on the 2020 Census operations, see U.S. Census Bureau (2018).
Some ongoing improvement operations are designed to deal with MAF errors, while others aim
to keep pace with post-census address development. In the remainder of this section, we discuss
several of these operations, including DSF updates, the Geographic Support System Initiative,
and field listing operations. Table 3-1 summarizes the development and improvement activities.
Table 3-1. Master Address File Development and Improvement
Initial Input (2000 and earlier) Improvements (Post-2000)

USPS Delivery Sequence File (DSF)

Delivery Sequence File
The DSF is the USPS’s master list of all delivery-point addresses served by postal carriers. The
file contains specific data coded for each record, a standardized address and ZIP code, and codes
that indicate how the address is served by mail delivery (for example, carrier route and the
sequential order in which the address is serviced on that route). The DSF record for a particular
address also includes a code for delivery type that indicates whether the address is a business or
residential. The DSF is the primary source of new city-style addresses used to update the MAF

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-6
between decennial censuses. DSF addresses are not used to update non-city-style addresses in the
MAF because those addresses might provide different and unmatchable address representations
for HUs whose addresses already exist in the MAF. New versions of the DSF are shared with the
Census Bureau monthly, and updates or “refreshes” to the MAF are made twice a year.
When DSF updates do not match an existing MAF record, a new record is created in the MAF.
These new records, which could be new housing units, are then compared to the USPS Locatable
Address Conversion Service (LACS) file, which indicates whether the new record is a change to
an existing address. In this way, the process can identify duplicate records for the same
address.13F
14
Field Listing Operations
Nationwide field listing operations are undertaken between censuses to address coverage
concerns. In a listing operation, a defined land area—usually a census tabulation block—is
traveled in a systematic manner, while a field representative (FR) records the location and
address of every structure where a person lives or could live. The Census Bureau conducts
listings using laptop computers with a specially designed application called the Listing and
Mapping Application (LiMA). The LiMA uses extracts from the current MTdb as inputs.
Functionality in the LiMA allows FRs to edit, add, delete, and verify addresses, streets, and other
map features; view a list of addresses associated with the assigned geography; and view and
denote the location of HUs on the electronic map. Global Positioning System (GPS) functionality
in the LiMA enables FRs to collect latitude and longitude coordinates for the structure.
Compared to information once collected by paper and pencil, the LiMA allows for the
standardization of data collected through edits and defined data entry fields, standardization of
field procedures, efficiencies in data transfer, and timely reflection of the address and feature
updates in the MTdb.
The Community Address Updating System (CAUS), Demographic Area Address Listing
(DAAL), and the MAF Coverage Study are examples of nationwide field listing operations.
The Census Bureau designed the CAUS program specifically to address concerns about ACS
coverage. The Census Bureau recognized that the DSF, the primary source of ACS frame
updates, does not adequately account for changes in predominantly rural areas of the nation
where city-style addresses are generally not used for mail delivery. An automated field data
collection operation, CAUS, was designed to provide a rural counterpart to the update of city-
style addresses received from the DSF. It improved coverage of the ACS by (1) adding addresses
that exist but do not appear in the DSF; (2) adding non-city-style addresses in the DSF that do
not appear on the MAF; (3) adding addresses in the DSF that also appear in the MAF but are
14 For additional details on the MAF update process via the DSF, see Hilts (2005).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-7
erroneously excluded from the ACS frame; and (4) deleting addresses that appear in the MAF
but are erroneously included in the ACS frame.
Implemented in September 2003, CAUS focused its efforts on census blocks with high
concentrations of non-city-style addresses and suspected growth in the HU inventory. Of the
approximately 8.2 million blocks nationwide, the CAUS universe comprised the 750,000 blocks
where DSF updates were not used to provide adequate coverage. The Census Bureau selected
CAUS blocks using a model-based method that used information gained from previous field data
collection efforts and administrative records to predict where CAUS work was needed. The
CAUS program was suspended from October 2007 to March 2010 until the 2010 Census
Address Canvassing and field follow-up activities were completed. The CAUS program resumed
listing activities again in April 2010. Approximately 36,000 blocks were listed from October
2010 until 2016, when CAUS was suspended again.14F
15
DAAL is a combination of operations, systems, and procedures associated with coverage
improvement, address list development, and automated listing for the CAUS and demographic
household surveys. The objective of DAAL is to update the inventory of HUs, GQs, and street
features in preparation for sample selection for the ACS and surveys such as the Current
Population Survey (CPS), the American Housing Survey (AHS), and the Survey of Income and
Program Participation (SIPP). DAAL was a major source of intercensal updates to the MAF until
it was suspended after the current surveys changed to a MAF-based sample design.15F
16
The MAF Coverage Study was a component of Address Canvassing for the 2020 Census.
Implemented in 2016, the MAF Coverage Study listed a nationwide sample of 18,500 blocks
using the LiMA. Updates in those blocks were applied to the MAF and used to produce estimates
of MAF coverage.16F
17
Geographic Support System Initiative
The Geography Division of the U.S. Census Bureau initiated a broad-based geographic support
system initiative in preparation for the 2020 Census. The initiative covered many aspects of
geographic support for these programs, including investigating various partnering opportunities
with local governments and pursuing commercial resources and crowdsourcing to maintain the
MTdb throughout the decade. This program improved coverage of the MAF by adding new HUs
and updating block geocodes for existing HUs.
15 For details on the CAUS program and its block selection methodology, see Hartman (2009, 2011) and Schar
(2012a, 2012b).
16 For details on DAAL, see Perrone (2005). For information about the current surveys redesign, see Kennel et al.
(2011).
17 For more information on the MAF Coverage Study, see Williams (2018).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-8
All of these MAF improvement activities and operations contribute to the overall update of the
MTdb.
3.4 Master Address File Development and Updating for Puerto Rico
The Census Bureau created an initial MAF for Puerto Rico through field listing operations. This
MAF did not include mailing addresses because, in Puerto Rico, the 2000 Census used an
Update/Leave methodology through which a census questionnaire was delivered by an
enumerator to each living quarter. The MAF update activities that took place from 2002 to 2004
were focused on developing mailing addresses, updating address information, and improving
coverage through yearly updates.
MAF Development in Puerto Rico
MAF development in Puerto Rico also used the 2000 Census operations as its foundation. These
operations in Puerto Rico included address listing, Update/Leave, the LUCA program, and the
Be Counted Campaign.17F
18
The Census Bureau designed the 2000 Census procedures and processing systems to capture,
process, transfer, and store information for the conventional three-line mailing address. Mailing
addresses in Puerto Rico generally incorporate the urbanization name (a geographic area roughly
equivalent to a neighborhood), which creates a four-line address. Using the urbanization name
eliminates the confusion created when street names are repeated in adjacent communities. In
some instances, the urbanization name is used in lieu of the street name.
The differences between the standard three-line address and the four-line format used in Puerto
Rico created problems during the early MAF building stages. The resulting file structure for the
Puerto Rico MAF was the same as that used for states in the United States, so it did not contain
the additional fields required to handle the more complex Puerto Rico mailing address. These
processing problems did not adversely impact the 2000 Census operations in the U.S. because
the record structure was designed to accommodate the standard U.S. three-line address.
However, in Puerto Rico, where questionnaire mailout was originally planned as the primary
means of collecting data, the three-line address format turned out to be problematic.
MAF Improvement Activities and Operations in Puerto Rico
Because of these address formatting issues, the MAF for Puerto Rico as it existed at the
conclusion of the 2000 Census required significant work before it could be used to fully
implement the Puerto Rico Community Survey (PRCS) starting in 2005. The Census Bureau had
to revise the address information in the Puerto Rico MAF. This effort involved splitting the
18 For details on the 2000 Census for Puerto Rico, see U.S. Census Bureau (2004).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-9
address information into the various fields required to construct a mailing address using Puerto
Rico addressing conventions.
The Census Bureau contracted for updating the list of addresses in the Puerto Rico MAF.
Approximately 64,000 new Puerto Rico HUs were added to the MAF, with each address
geocoded to a municipio, tract, and block. The Census Bureau also worked with the USPS DSF
for Puerto Rico to extract information on new HU addresses. Matching the USPS file to the
existing MAF was only partially successful because of inconsistent naming conventions, missing
information in the MAF, and the existence of different house numbering schemes (USPS versus
local schemes). Data collection activities for the 2005 PRCS began in November 2004 with the
best address information available given these shortcomings.
In preparation for the 2010 Census, the Census Bureau conducted address canvassing in Puerto
Rico as it was done in the United States, updating the inventory of housing units in the MAF for
Puerto Rico before the 2010 Census. Results from the 2010 Census Update/Leave and follow-up
operations also updated the MAF addresses in Puerto Rico. The Census Bureau determined the
final 2010 Census status of each HU record in the MAF in Puerto Rico in late 2010.
As part of the MAF/TIGER redesign effort before 2010, the Census Bureau redesigned the MAF
to accommodate the Puerto Rico-specific address components that were lacking previously. The
MAF now accommodates these specific address components, allowing the potential to update the
MAF in Puerto Rico by census field operations and other methods. In spring 2016, the Census
Bureau updated the MAF with DSF addresses in Puerto Rico for the first time.
Address Canvassing was not done in Puerto Rico before the 2020 Census. The 2020 Census was
conducted by visiting every housing unit in Puerto Rico during the Update/Leave operation.
Geocoded DSF addresses in Puerto Rico were included in this operation. Addresses counted in
the 2020 Census comprise the majority of the addresses on the PRCS frame after the 2020
Census.
3.5 Master Address File Development and Updating for Group Quarters in
the United States and Puerto Rico
MAF Development for GQs
In preparation for the 2000 Census, the Census Bureau developed an inventory of special places
(SPs) and GQs. SPs are facilities such as prisons, hotels, migrant farm camps, and universities.
GQs are contained within SPs and include college and university dormitories and hospital/prison
wards. The SP/GQ inventory was developed using data from internal Census Bureau lists,
administrative lists obtained from various federal agencies, and numerous Census 2000
operations such as address listing, block canvassing, and the SP/GQ Facility Questionnaire
operation. Responses to the SP/GQ Facility Questionnaire identified GQs and any HUs

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-10
associated with the SP. Similar to the HU MAF development process, local and tribal
governments had an opportunity to review the SP address list. In August 2000, after the
enumeration of GQ facilities, the Census Bureau incorporated the address and identification
information for each GQ into the MAF.
MAF Improvement Activities and Operations for GQs
Maintenance of the GQ universe is an ongoing and complex task. The earlier section on MAF
Improvement Activities and Operations for HUs mentions short-term/one-time operations that
also updated GQ information. Additionally, the Census Bureau completed a GQ geocoding
correction operation to fix errors (mostly census block geocodes) associated with college
dormitories in the MTdb.
The Census Bureau may identify new GQ facilities and update address information for existing
ones during field listing operations. This information is used to update the MAF. Additionally, it
is likely that DSF updates of city-style address areas are providing the Census Bureau with new
GQ addresses; however, the DSF does not identify such an address as a GQ facility.
Prior to 2010 Census operations, the Census Bureau developed a process to supplement these
activities to create an updated GQ universe from which to select the ACS sample. The Census
Bureau constructed the ACS GQ universe by merging the updated SP/GQ inventory file, extracts
from the MAF, and a file of those seasonal GQs that were closed on April 1, 2000 (but might
have been open if visited at another time of year). To supplement the ACS GQ universe, the
Census Bureau obtained a file of federal prisons and detention centers from the Bureau of
Prisons (BOP) and a file from the Department of Defense (DOD) containing military bases and
vessels. The Census Bureau also conducted research to identify new migrant worker locations,
new state prisons, and state prisons that had closed.
ACS FRs, while administering the Group Quarters Facility Questionnaire (GQFQ), collect
updated address and geographic location information. Updates collected via the GQFQ were
used to provide more accurate information for subsequent facility visits and update the ACS GQ
universe. For more information about the GQFQ, see the Group Quarters (Facility-Level Phase)
section in Section 8.2 of Chapter 8.
The Address Canvassing operation for the 2010 Census identified records as “other living
quarters” or OLQs. All OLQs and GQs were then visited during the Group Quarters Validation
operation, where their final status as a HU or GQ was determined. GQs were then enumerated in
the GQ Enumeration operation. The Census Bureau applied updates from all of these operations
to the MAF. The Census Bureau determined the final 2010 Census status for each GQ in late
2010.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-11
The final 2010 Census universe of GQs is the basis of the ACS GQ frame for 2012 through
2021. ACS also includes GQs that were identified as having no population on Census Day, as
those GQs may contain people if visited at another time of the year. New GQs from ongoing
operations are also included in the ACS GQ frame. The Census Bureau updates the ACS GQ
frame and the MAF with results from ACS GQ data collection operations as well as results of
state prison research using the individual state Department of Corrections websites. ACS
continues to partner with the BOP to ensure the most accurate sampling frame for federal
prisons.18F
19
Instead of identifying addresses as OLQs, the 2020 Address Canvassing operation was able to
convert addresses to GQs. Any known GQs after Address Canvassing were included in the 2020
Census GQ Advance Contact operation. This operation attempted to contact known GQ facilities
by phone or personal visit to obtain updated information to assist with the GQ enumeration
process. The universe of GQs enumerated as part of the 2020 Census became the foundation of
the ACS GQ frame for the data year 2022 after the census had been completed.
3.6 American Community Survey Extracts from the Master Address File
Data from the MTdb are provided for use in the ACS in files called MAF extracts. These MAF
extracts contain a subset of the data items in the MAF. The major classifications of variables
included in the MAF extracts are address variables, geocode variables, and source and status
variables (see Section 3.2).
As an inventory of living quarters (HUs and GQs) and some non-residential units, the MAF is a
dynamic entity. It contains hundreds of millions of addresses that reflect ongoing additions,
deletions, and changes; these include current addresses as well as those determined to no longer
exist. Each Census Bureau program that relies on the MAF defines the set of valid addresses for
its individual program.
Since the ACS frame must be as complete as possible, the Census Bureau applies filtering rules
during the creation of the ACS extracts to minimize both overcoverage and undercoverage and to
obtain an inclusive listing of addresses. For example, the ACS filter rules include units that
represent new construction units, some of which may not exist yet. The ACS also includes other
housing units that are not geocoded, meaning that the address has not yet been linked to a census
tract and block. In addition, the ACS includes units that are “excluded from delivery statistics”
(EDS); these units often are those under construction (i.e., the housing unit is being constructed
and has an address, but the USPS is not yet delivering mail to the address).
19 For more information on the post-2010 Census ACS GQ frame, see Bates (2020).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 3: Frame Development
3-12
Filtering rules change as new, vetted operations update the MAF. The post-2020 ACS frame
consists of 2020 Census addresses plus any new records added to the MAF after the 2020
Census, including post-Census DSF adds; new or validated records from listing operations; new
addresses from CQR and the Geographic Support System Initiative; and 2020 Census deletes
that persist on the DSF.
As discussed above, the ACS attempts to create a sampling frame that is as accurate as possible
by minimizing both overcoverage and undercoverage.19F
20 In the process, the ACS filter rules can
lead to net overcoverage, reflecting some duplicate and nonexistent units. This overcoverage has
been estimated to be approximately 1.9 to 5.2 percent from 2002–2009. See Kephart (2010) for a
discussion of this issue.20F
21
20 Definitions of the terms “overcoverage” and “undercoverage” are provided in the Glossary. For details on how
the ACS publishes estimates of overcoverage and undercover, see Chapter 15.
21 For details on the ACS requirements for MAF extracts, see La Kose and Bates (2019). For information on the
ACS sample selection, see Chapter 4. For a description of data collection procedures for Hus and GQs, see Chapters
7 and 8, respectively. For details on the MAF, its coverage, and the implications of extract rules on the ACS frame,
see Shapiro and Waksberg (1999) and Kephart (2010).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-1
Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4.1 Overview
The American Community Survey (ACS) and Puerto Rico Community Survey (PRCS) each
consist of two separate samples: housing unit (HU) addresses and residents of group quarters
(GQ) facilities. As described in Chapter 3, we derive the sampling frames from which we draw
these samples from the Census Bureau’s Master Address File (MAF). The MAF is one
component of the combined database that combines both address information from the MAF
with spatial information containing the geographic boundaries from the Topologically Integrated
Geographic Encoding and Referencing (TIGER) database. The combined MAF/TIGER database
is used both for sampling and tabulation for the ACS as well as most Census surveys and data
products. The MAF is the Census Bureau’s official inventory of known living quarters and
selected nonresidential units in the United States and Puerto Rico.
We select independent HU address samples for each of the 3,143 counties and county
equivalents in the U.S., including the District of Columbia, as well as for each of the 78
municipalities in Puerto Rico. In 2004, we selected samples of HU addresses for every county
and county equivalent for field data collection in 2005.21F
22 Each year from 2005–2010, we selected
approximately 2.9 million HU addresses in the U.S. and 36,000 HU addresses in Puerto Rico.
Beginning in 2011, we implemented the following changes to the ACS sample design:
• We increased the HU sample in June 2011, bringing the size of the sample selected
to 3.54 million addresses per year.
• We added several new HU sampling rates that allow better control of the allocation
of the sample and improve estimate reliability for small areas.
• We increased the follow-up sample to 100 percent in select geographic areas.
In addition, starting in 2013, we restricted the assignment of the GQ sample for college dorms to
the non-summer months (January–April, September–December).
Since 2013, we have instituted several changes to how the housing unit address sample is
selected. Most notably, beginning with the 2016 sample selection that occurred in September of
2015, we moved to selecting two independent six-month samples for a given year, each
consisting of roughly half of the target sample size of 3.54 million. The first sample, referred to
as Period 1, is selected from the July delivery of the Master Address File extracts during
September, and these addresses are assigned to January–June of the sample year. The second
sample, referred to as Period 2, is selected from the addresses on the January delivery of the
MAF extracts during March and is assigned to the July–December panels. All addresses on the
22 In the remainder of this chapter, the term “county” refers to counties, county equivalents, and municipalities.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-2
July delivery and that are in the current year’s subframe22F
23 are assigned to one of the two
sampling periods (Period 1 or Period 2). During Period 2, we use this pre-designation of period
for the addresses processed during Period 1 and that remain valid, and all new addresses are
randomly assigned to either Period 1 or Period 2. Essentially, all the steps of the sampling are performed twice a year. In addition to the change to
two six-month samples, all blocks are now assigned to a sampling stratum twice a year. This is a
further improvement in the design, as any changes in the size of the set of geographic areas used
in the sampling are now reflected every six months instead of only once a year. Beginning in 2018, an improvement in sampling efficiency was made by introducing the concept
of “Eligible for Data Collection.” Based on feedback from the Field Division and research into
the make-up of certain addresses in the MAF extracts, all addresses must now have a minimum
set of information to be eligible for second-stage sampling23F
24 , and thus for data collection.24F
25
Addresses without this minimum set of information were difficult to contact via mail and
difficult to locate in the personal visit data collection. Thus, they often were resolved as a
nonresponse despite being unable to locate them, as there was no direct evidence that they were
invalid housing units. These sample cases were, in essence, wasted, given that we could
reasonably predict our inability to contact these addresses. To improve efficiency, therefore, only
addresses deemed to be valid and now flagged as “Eligible for Data Collection” (satisfying the
minimum set of information necessary to contact them) are eligible to be selected for the ACS. Since a number of the addresses flagged as “not eligible” are still flagged as “valid” or “good”,
there is an adjustment to the sampling weight to account for these addresses, and the weights of
the sampled addresses still represent all “valid” or “good” addresses on the frame, as they always
have. Full-implementation samples of GQ facilities and persons began in 2006 and are selected
independently within each state, including the District of Columbia and Puerto Rico.

nt for these addresses, and the weights of
the sampled addresses still represent all “valid” or “good” addresses on the frame, as they always
have. Full-implementation samples of GQ facilities and persons began in 2006 and are selected
independently within each state, including the District of Columbia and Puerto Rico. In 2006 and
2007, the ACS and the PRCS included approximately 2.5 percent of the expected number of
residents in GQ facilities. Beginning in 2008, we increased the sampling rates in 16 states with
small GQ populations to meet publication reliability thresholds. See Chapter 8 for details on the
GQ data collection methods. This chapter details the selection of the HU address and GQ person samples. As the ACS is a
continuous survey, periodic refinements are made to the sampling methodology over time. For
this chapter, we focus on the methodology used to select the 2022 sample as our reference date to
coincide with the last standard 1-year data release at the time that this chapter was drafted. The
final section describes the differences in sampling and data collection methodologies for some
23 This document explains the concept of subframes in detail in section 4.3
24 Addresses that are valid, but not eligible for data collection, are only eligible for first-stage sampling. 25 For more details regarding the minimum set of information required for addresses to be eligible for data
collection, see Bates (2021).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-3
hard-to-reach areas in Alaska (referred to as Remote Alaska). The section on Remote Alaska also
details recently modified sampling and data collection procedures for these areas.
4.2 Housing Unit Sample Selection
There are two phases of HU address sampling for each county.25F
26 During the first phase of
sampling, we assign blocks to sampling strata, calculate sampling rates, and select the initial
sample. During the second phase of sampling, we select a sample of nonresponding addresses for
Computer Assisted Personal Interviewing (CAPI). This is referred to as the CAPI sample.
First-phase sampling produces the annual ACS initial sample of addresses and includes two
processes—Period 1 and Period 2 sampling. The Period 1 and Period 2 samples in the first-phase
sampling each include two stages. The first-stage sample selection systematically assigns new
addresses to one of five subframes and identifies the appropriate subframe associated with a
specific year’s sample. The second-stage sample selection systematically selects the sample from
the selected subframe.
Figure 4-1 provides a visual overview of the housing unit address sampling process described in
the previous paragraph.
26 For first-stage HU sampling, “addresses” refers to valid ACS addresses. For second-stage HU sampling, “addresses”
refers to valid ACS addresses that are eligible for data collection.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-4
Figure 4-1. Overview of Sampling Process

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-5
4.3 First-Phase Sample
The first phase of sampling is comprised of two separate stages. The first stage of first-phase
sampling maintains five distinct partitions, or subframes, of the addresses on the sampling frame
within each county. Each county subframe is a representative sample of all valid addresses in the
county. The subframes are assigned to specific years and are rotated annually. The subframes
maintain their annual designation over time. First-stage sampling systematically sorts and assigns
addresses that are new to the frame to one of the five subframes.26F
27 First-stage sampling also
affects the second-stage sampling rates for each stratum for the current sample year.
The second stage of first-phase sampling selects a sample of the addresses from the current
year’s subframe and allocates this sample to the twelve months of the year for data collection.
Determining the First-Phase Sampling Rates Overview
Having a fixed 3.54 million annual sample, the ACS must recalculate the sampling rates each
year that will achieve that fixed sample when applied to a sampling frame that generally grows
over time. To that end, we define sixteen sampling strata for the first-phase sampling where three
of the strata have fixed sampling rates and the remaining 13 strata have variable sampling rates
that allow us to meet our sample size constraint. Each of these variable sampling rates is a
multiple of a common base rate (BR). Thus, by adjusting the BR, we can adjust the size of the
overall sample while maintaining a fixed relationship between the sampling rates for the variable
sampling rate strata.
The first step in this process is to assign each address from the sampling frame for the period to a
first-phase sampling stratum. Next, these universe counts by sampling strata are used to derive
the individual stratum sampling rates that will achieve the target sample. The details for each of
these steps are described in the subsequent sections.
Assigning Blocks and Addresses to the First-Phase Sampling Strata
Each year and for each Period, the sampling operation assigns each block to one of the sixteen
sampling strata and, consequently, assigns each block one of sixteen sampling rates.27F
28 The ACS
produces estimates for geographic areas having a wide range of population sizes. To ensure that
the estimates for these areas have the desired level of reliability, we must sample areas with
smaller populations at higher rates relative to those with larger populations. We base the stratum
assignment for a block on information about a set of specific geographic entities—referred to as
27 All existing addresses retain their previous assignment to one of the five subframes. The five subframes are
maintained to meet the requirement that no address be in sample more than once in any five-year period.
28 From 2005−2010, five sampling strata were used where one used a fixed rate and the other strata had a variable
rate.

Sampling entities are:
• Counties
• School districts
• American Indian areas/Alaska Native areas/Hawaiian Home Lands (AIANHH)
• American Indian Tribal Subdivisions with active and functioning governments
• Minor civil divisions (MCDs) with active and functioning governments in 12
states28F

We base the sampling stratum assignment for most blocks, at least in part, on the measure of size
(MOS) for the smallest sampling entity to which any part of the block belongs. To calculate the
MOS for a sampling entity, we derive block-level counts of addresses from the sampling frame.
This count is converted to an estimated number of occupied HUs by multiplying it by the
proportion of occupied HUs in the block in the 2020 Census. For American Indian and Alaska
Native Areas (AIANA31F
32 ) including Tribal Subdivisions, we multiply the estimated number of
occupied HUs by the proportion of its population that responded as American Indian or Alaska
Native (either alone or in combination) in the 2020 Census.32F
33 For each sampling entity, we sum
these estimates across all blocks in the entity to create the MOS for the entity. In AIANAs, if the
sum of these estimates across all blocks is nonzero, then this sum becomes the MOS for the
AIANA. If it is zero (because of a zero-census count of American Indians or Alaska Natives), the
occupied HU estimate for the AIANA is the MOS for the AIANA. For more details, see the
computer specifications for calculating the MOS for the ACS (Keathley, 2022a). We assign each

Indiana Areas Program for the 2020 Census, see the publication in the Federal Register (U.S. Census Bureau, 2008b).

Census (U.S. Census Bureau, 2024).

block the smallest MOS of all the sampling entities in which the block is contained, and we refer
to it as the Smallest Entity Measure of Size, or SEMOS.
If the SEMOS is greater than or equal to 1,200, we base the stratum assignment for the block on
the MOS for the census tract that contains it as well as historical survey response rates prior to
CAPI data collection. The tract measure of size (TMOS) is calculated as the sum of the estimated
number of occupied HUs across all blocks contained within it. The survey response rates used
measure the completed questionnaires across all modes before CAPI as a proportion of the
sample that was eligible (estimated occupied mailable addresses).
This adjustment is to compensate for the increase in costs because of increasing the CAPI
sampling rates in tracts predicted to have the lowest rate of completed interviews in the pre-
CAPI modes. Note that the initial identification of these tracts, performed in 2004, was used in
the 2005 sample selection and was revised in 2007 based on more recent data. It has been used
since the 2008 sample selection. At that time, we used a combined mail and Computer Assisted
Telephone Interview rate. A threshold of 60 percent was determined through research to be a
tipping point between the higher and lower response rate tracts (Asiala, 2005).
Using SEMOS, TMOS, and the tract-level historical response rates, we can assign blocks to the
sixteen strata defined in columns 1 and 2 in Table 4-1 on the following page.

Example 1: Suppose the MOS for City A is 600 and the MOS for MCD A is 1,100. Then the

Example 2: Suppose the MOS for City A is 1,300, the MOS for MCD A is 300 and the MOS for
