SEMOS for the Census Block is 300 and it is placed in the 200 ≤ SEMOS < 400 stratum. In this
example, a different census block with City A that was contained in MCD B would likely fall

not be uniform across blocks within a given sampling entity.

the Tract 1 and the predicted level of completed interviews prior to CAPI sampling in the tract.
In this example, suppose the TMOS is 1,800, and the predicted level of completed interviews
prior to CAPI sampling is ≤ 60 percent, then the Census Block will be placed in the
1,000 < TMOS ≤ 2,000 stratum with a predicted level of completed interviews prior to CAPI

Determine the Base Rate and Calculating the First-Phase Sampling Stratum Rates
Each year, we must determine the specific set of sampling rates for each of the thirteen non-fixed
rate sampling strata defined in Table 4-1. Before we can do this, we must perform the following
two steps. The first step is to calculate a base rate (BR) for the current year. Thirteen of the
sixteen sampling rates are a function of a base rate. The three fixed rate strata are 15 percent, 10
percent, and 7 percent. Column 3 of Table 4-1 shows the relationship between the base rate and
the sixteen sampling rates.
After having assigned each address from the sampling frame for the period to a first-phase
sampling stratum, universe counts for each stratum are tallied. These counts are then multiplied
by the stratum sampling rates (which are a function of BR) and an algebraic expression for the
total sample size, as a function of the BR, is obtained. Setting this expression equal to the fixed
period sample size of 1.77 million (each period selects half of the annual sample) and solving
determines the BR for the period. While this is done independently for each period, in practice
the BRs for each period differ only slightly from one another, since the growth in the MAF from
period to period is small relative to the total size of the sampling frame.
As an effect of having a fixed sample size, we reduce the sampling rates for all but the smallest
sampling entity strata (SEMOS < 800) each year as the number of addresses in the U.S. and
Puerto Rico increases. However, as shown in Table 4-1, among the strata where the rates are
decreasing, the relationship between the sampling rates will remain proportionally constant. The
sampling rates for the smallest sampling entities will remain at 15 percent, 10 percent, and 7
percent.
For the blocks that fall into higher response rate tracts, we multiply the sampling rates by 0.92
(reduced by 8 percent) for the six strata in which the SEMOS was greater than or equal to 1,200
to offset the costs of the increased second-phase sampling rates.
Because of this adjustment, there are sixteen sampling rates used in the U.S., and ten in Puerto
Rico, as shown in columns 2 and 3 of Table 4-1. See the research report (Asiala, 2005) for a full
description of the relationship between this reduction and the CAPI sampling rates. This
reduction does not occur in Puerto Rico; therefore, there are ten sampling strata eligible to be
used in Puerto Rico. Table 4-2 shows the final sampling rates used for 2022 after our final
calculations. Note that only five strata in Puerto Rico contain valid addresses on the 2022 Period
1 sampling frame, so for 2022, we only used the five sampling rates shown in Table 4-2.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-13
First-Stage Sampling: Random Assignment of Addresses to a Specific Year
One of the ACS design requirements is that no HU address be in sample more than once in any
five-year period in order to reduce respondent burden. To accommodate this constraint, the
addresses in the frame are assigned systematically to five subframes, each containing roughly 20
percent of the frame and each being a representative sample. Addresses from only one of these
subframes are eligible to be in the ACS sample each year, and each subframe is used every fifth
year. For example, 2022 will have the same addresses in its subframe as did 2017, with the
addition of all new addresses that we assigned to that subframe during the 2017–2022 time
period. Therefore, both the Period 1 and Period 2 sample selection occur in two stages. The first
stage partitions addresses new to the sampling frame into the five subframes and determines the
subframe for the current year. The second stage, described in more detail below, selects
addresses to be included in the ACS from the subframe eligible for the sample year (Keathley,
2022b).
Before the 2005 sample selection, there was a one-time allocation of all addresses then present
on the ACS frame into the five subframes. In subsequent years, addresses new to the frame are
systematically allocated to these five subframes. This is accomplished by sorting the addresses in
each county by stratum and geographic order, including tract, block, street name, house number,
and MAF Identification Number (MAFID). Addresses are then sequentially assigned to each of
the five existing subframes. This procedure is similar to the use of systematic sampling with a
sampling interval of five, in which the first address in the interval is randomly assigned to one of
the subframes, the second address in the interval is assigned to the next year (in order), and so
on. Specifically, during Period 1 sampling, only the addresses new to the MAF since the
previous year’s Period 2 sample selection are eligible for first-stage sampling and go through the
process of assignment to a subframe. Similarly, during Period 2 sampling, only addresses new to
the MAF since Period 1 sampling go through first-stage sampling.
Assignment of Addresses to Period 1 or Period 2
After first-stage sampling is complete for Period 1, all addresses on the current year’s subframe
are then allocated to Period 1 or Period 2. The addresses assigned to Period 2 will wait for
further processing in the later period. Those addresses assigned to Period 1 will then move on to
second-stage sampling for the period.
After first-stage sampling is complete for Period 2, all addresses new since Period 1 that are on
the subframe will similarly be assigned to Period 1 or Period 2, although only those randomly
assigned to Period 2 will go on to second-stage sampling for the given year. Addresses in the
current subframe that existed at the time of Period 1 sampling and were assigned to Period 2 will
also go on to second-stage sampling for Period 2.
The Period 1 sample will be used for the January–June monthly samples, and the Period 2
sample will be used for the July–December monthly samples.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-14
Second-Stage Sampling: Selection of Addresses
With the first-stage sampling and period assignment complete, the second-stage sampling selects
the entire first-phase sample from the current year’s subframe for the given period. Thus, the
second-stage sampling rates need to account for the first-stage sampling rates to achieve our
target first-phase rates. These second-stage sampling rates are approximately equal to the final
sampling rate divided by 20 percent.33F
34
The second-stage sample is selected using systematic sampling after applying a geographic sort
within sampling stratum. The sample selection is performed independently by county using
systematic sampling from the portion of the subframe allocated to the current period. As noted
earlier, the selection of the Period 1 and Period 2 samples together comprise the entire annual
ACS HU sample.
Second-Stage Sampling: Sample Month Assignment for Address Samples
We must assign each sample address for a particular year to a specific data collection month. The
set of all addresses assigned to a specific month is the month’s sample or panel. We sort
addresses selected by stratum and geography and assign them systematically to the first six
months or the last six months of the year, depending on the Period. However, we assign
addresses that one of several Census Bureau household surveys have also selected to an ACS
data collection month based on the interview month(s) for these other household surveys.34F
35 The
goal of the assignments is to reduce the respondent burden of completing interviews for both the
ACS and another survey during the same month.
4.4 Second-Phase Sampling for CAPI Follow-up
The ACS uses three modes of data collection—internet, mail, and personal visits. (see Chapter 7
for more information on HU data collection). Mailable sample addresses are eligible to complete
the survey during the entire three-month time period. Cases without a completed internet or mail
questionnaire are eligible for CAPI in the third month, as are the unmailable addresses (this
category includes addresses in remote Alaska). An address is unmailable if the address is
incomplete or directs mail to only a post office box. Table 4-3 summarizes the eligibility of
addresses for CAPI sampling.
34 The second-stage rate is approximately equal to the sampling rate divided by 20 percent since the first-stage
sampling rate is approximately 20 percent, and the first-stage rate times the second-stage rate equals the overall
sampling rate. An adjustment is made to account for uneven distributions of addresses in the county-level
subframes.
35 These surveys include the Survey of Income and Program Participation, the National Crime Victimization Survey, the
Consumer Expenditures Quarterly and Diary Surveys, the Current Population Survey, the State Child Health Insurance
Program Surveys, and the American Housing Survey (in odd-numbered years only).

The CAPI sample selects a systematic sample of these addresses for CAPI data collection each
month using the rates shown in Table 4-4. The selection is made after sorting within county by
CAPI sampling rate and geographic order within the address frame (Bullock, 2022a).
The variance of estimates of HUs and people living in them for a given area is a function of the
number of interviews completed within that area. However, because of the subsampling, CAPI
cases generally have larger weights than cases completed by internet or mail. The variance of the
estimates for an area will tend to increase as the proportion of internet and mail responses
decreases. Large differences in these proportions across areas of similar size may result in
substantial differences in the reliability of their estimates. To minimize this possibility, tracts in
the U.S. that are predicted to have low levels of interviews completed by internet or mail have
their CAPI sampling rates adjusted upward from the default 1-in-3 rate for mailable addresses.
This tends to reduce variances for the affected areas both by potentially increasing their total
numbers of completed interviews and by decreasing the differences in weights between their
CAPI and non-CAPI interviews.
No information was available to reliably predict the levels of completed interviews prior to
second-phase sampling for CAPI follow-up in Puerto Rico before 2005, so we initially used the
sampling rates of 1-in-3 for mailable and 2-in-3 for unmailable addresses. Based on early
response results observed during the first months of the PRCS, we changed the CAPI sampling
rate for mailable addresses in all Puerto Rico tracts to 1-in-2 beginning in June 2005.
We made several enhancements to the CAPI sampling, beginning with the 2011 sample, to
increase the reliability of the ACS estimates for populations in certain well-defined geographic
areas. Beginning in January of 2011, we send all Remote Alaska sample addresses to CAPI
where previously they had been sampled at the rate assigned to unmailable cases (2-in-3). In
addition, we do not subsample CAPI-eligible addresses that are either unmailable or that did not
respond via internet or mail in the following areas:
• Hawaiian Home Lands
• Alaska Native Village Statistical Areas

Census reporting their race as American Indian or Alaska Native (alone or in
combination).

4.5 Group Quarters Sample Selection
GQ facilities include such places as college residence halls, residential treatment centers, skilled
nursing facilities, group homes, military barracks, correctional facilities, workers’ dormitories,
and facilities for people experiencing homelessness. We classify each GQ facility according to
its GQ type. (For more information on GQ facilities, see Chapter 8.) As noted previously, the
2005 ACS did not include GQ facilities, but we have included GQs since 2006. We select the
GQ sample for a given year during two operations. Federal prisons managed by BOP are
sampled in July of the sample year and all other GQs are sampled in October of the previous
year. The MAF extract delivered in July of the previous year defines the sampling frame for the
primary GQ sampling. Since 2006, the Census Bureau and the Federal Bureau of Prisons (BOP)
have had an inter-agency agreement, and the BOP has delivered the most recent list of federal
prisons (managed by the BOP) as well as the most recent roster of federal inmates in those
prisons to the Census Bureau. From 2006 to 2015, the BOP sampling identified the number of
people to sample during the same procedure as the non-BOP sample. Beginning with the 2016

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-17
sampling, a separate sampling frame was created using the facilities file delivered from the BOP,
and the BOP sample was selected from this frame. The ultimate sampling units for the GQ
sample are the GQ residents, not the facilities. The GQ samples are independent state-level
samples.
The ACS sampling and data collection operations exclude certain GQ types, including domestic
violence shelters, soup kitchens, regularly scheduled mobile food vans, targeted non-sheltered
outdoor locations, commercial maritime vessels, natural disaster shelters, and dangerous
encampments. There are several reasons for their exclusion, varying by GQ type. Concerns about
privacy and the operational feasibility of repeated interviewing for a continuing survey, rather
than once a decade for a census, led to the decision to exclude these GQ types. However, we
control ACS estimates of the total population to be consistent with the Population Estimates
Program estimate of the GQ resident population from all GQs, even those excluded from the
ACS. Overall, the excluded GQ types contain a relatively small proportion of the total GQ
population.
We classify all GQ facilities into one of two groups: (1) small GQ facilities (having 15 or fewer
people according to the 2020 Census or updated information) and (2) large GQ facilities (with an
expected population of more than 15 people). There are approximately 115,000 small GQ
facilities and 79,000 large GQ facilities on the 2022 GQ sampling frame and 189 BOP facilities.
We create two sampling strata to sample the GQ facilities. The first stratum includes both small
GQ facilities and those with no available population count. The second stratum includes large
facilities. In the remainder of this chapter, these strata will be referred to as the small GQ stratum
and the large GQ stratum. We compute a GQ measure of size (GQMOS) for use in sampling the
large GQ facilities. The GQMOS for each large GQ is the expected population count divided by
10.
The sampling procedures differ for these two strata. We sample GQs in the small GQ stratum,
like addresses in the HU sample, and collect data for all people in the selected GQ facilities. Like
HU addresses, small GQ facilities are eligible to be in the sample only once in a five-year period.
For GQs in the large stratum, a subsample of the people living in the GQ is selected. The first
phase of sampling selects groups of 10 people (“hits”) for interview from GQ facilities in the
large GQ stratum, and the number of these groups selected for a large GQ facility is a function of
its GQMOS. Unlike HU addresses and small GQs, large GQ facilities are eligible for sampling
each year. For more detail, see the computer specifications for the GQ sampling (Bullock,
2022b).
4.6 Small Group Quarters Stratum Sample
For the small GQ stratum, a two-phase, two-stage sampling procedure is used. In the first phase,
we select a GQ facility sample using a method similar to that used for the first-phase HU address
sample. Just as we saw in the HU address sampling, the first phase has two stages. Stage 1
systematically assigns small GQ facilities to a subframe associated with a specific year. During

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-18
the second stage, we select a systematic sample of the small GQ facilities. In the second phase of
sampling, we interview all people in the facility as long as there are 15 or fewer at the time of
interview. Otherwise, we select and interview a subsample of 10 people.
First Phase of Small GQ Sampling—First Stage: Random Assignment of GQ Facilities to
Subframes
The sampling procedure for 2006 assigned all of the GQ facilities in the small stratum to one of
five subframes, each containing approximately 20 percent of the full frame. We sorted the GQ
facilities within each state by small versus closed on Census Day, new versus previously
existing, GQ type (such as skilled nursing facility, military barracks, or dormitory), and
geographical order (county, tract, block, street name, and GQ identifier) in the small GQ frame.
In each year after 2006, we assigned new GQ facilities systematically to the five subframes. The
subframe for the 2022 GQ sample selection contains the facilities previously designated to the
subframe for calendar year 2022 and 20 percent of new small GQ facilities added since 2021.
The small GQ facilities in the 2022 subframe will not be eligible for sampling again until 2027
since the once-in-five-year period restriction also applies to small GQ facilities.
First Phase of Small GQ Sampling—Second Stage: Selection of Facilities
The second-stage sample is a systematic sample of the GQ facilities from the assigned subframe
within each state. The GQs are sorted by the order in which they were selected during first-stage
sampling. Because the range of sizes for these GQs is relatively narrow, we use the same
probability of selection for all small GQs regardless of their actual size. The second-stage
sampling rate combined with the 1-in-5 first-stage sampling rate yields an overall first-phase
sampling rate equal to the sampling rate for each state. As an example, if the sampling rate for
the state is 2.5 percent, then the second-stage sampling rate would be 1-in-8 so that the overall
GQ sampling rate would be (1-in-5) × (1-in-8) = 1-in-40 = 2.5 percent. Table 4-5 shows the 2022
state-level sampling rates.

Second Phase of Small GQ Sampling: Selection of Persons within Selected Facilities
Every person in the GQ facilities selected in this sample is eligible to be interviewed. If the
number of people in the GQ facility exceeds 15, interviewers perform a field subsampling
operation to reduce the total number of sampled people to 10, similar to the groups of 10 selected
in the large GQ stratum.
4.7 Large Group Quarters Stratum Sample
Unlike the HU address and small GQ samples, we do not divide the large GQ facilities into five
subframes. For field workload balance, we conduct interviews in groups of ten rather than
sampling all residents of the GQ facility. Like the small GQs, we use a two-phase sampling
procedure. The first phase indirectly selects the GQ facilities by selecting groups of ten within
the facilities. The second phase selects the actual ten people for each facility’s assigned group(s)
using the roster obtained from the facility at the time of interviewing. The number of groups

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-20
eligible to be sampled from a large GQ facility is equal to its GQMOS (equal to its estimated size
divided by 10). For example, if a facility had 550 people in the 2020 Census, its GQMOS is 55,
and 55 groups of ten are eligible for selection in the sample.
First Phase of Large GQ Sampling: Selection of Groups of Ten (and Associated Facilities)
We sort all of the large GQ facilities in a state by GQ type and geographical order in the large
GQ frame and select a systematic sample of groups of ten. For this reason, in states with a 2.5
percent sampling rate, a GQ facility with fewer than 40 groups (or roughly 400 individuals) may
or may not have one of its groups selected for the sample. GQ facilities in a state with a 2.5
percent sampling rate and between 40 and 80 groups will have at least one group selected with
certainty. If the GQ facility has between 80 and 120 groups, it will have at least two groups
selected, and so forth.
Second Phase of Large GQ Sampling: Selection of Persons within Facilities
For the non-BOP GQs, the second phase of sampling takes place within each large GQ facility
with at least one group selected in the first phase. When a field representative (FR) visits a GQ
facility to conduct interviews, an automated instrument randomly selects the ten people to be
included for each group of ten being interviewed. The instrument is pre-loaded with the number
of expected person interviews (ten times the number of groups selected) and a random starting
number. The FR then enters the actual number of people in the facility and a roster of their
names. To achieve a group size of 10, the instrument computes the appropriate sampling interval
based on the observed population at the time of interviewing. Then, it selects the actual people
for the interviews using a pre-loaded random start and a systematic algorithm. If the large GQ
has an observed population of 10 or more people, the instrument selects a group size of 10; if the
observed population is less than 10, the instrument selects everyone in the GQ.
For most GQ types, if multiple groups are selected within a GQ facility, their groups of ten are
assigned to different sample months for interviewing. Very large GQ facilities with more than 12
groups selected have multiple groups assigned to some sample months. In these cases, we are
able to avoid selecting the same person more than once in a sample month. However, there is no
attempt made to prevent the selection of someone more than once across sample months within a
year. Thus, we could interview someone in a very large GQ facility in consecutive months. All
GQ facilities in this stratum are eligible for selection every year, regardless of their sample status
in previous years.
The person sample for BOP GQs in sample is selected during the BOP sampling operation and is
drawn directly from the list of federal inmates on the roster file delivered by the BOP. Actual
names are selected for each hit, and the names of the selected inmates are pre-printed on paper
questionnaires, which the FR delivers to each BOP GQ in sample. The paper questionnaires are
distributed to the sampled inmates by staff in each prison and, after completion, are mailed to the
Census Bureau.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-21
4.8 Final Group Quarters Sampling Steps
In 2010, we instituted a procedure that guarantees each county has at least one GQ in sample in
any five-year period. This was driven by concerns about undercoverage of the GQ population in
some counties as a result of the inherent clustering of the GQ population combined with a
relatively small sampling interval. This final sampling step is performed after the selection of
both the small and large GQ stratum sampling is complete. It will select one GQ from any
county, without controlling for type, that has not had a GQ in sample over the past five years and
has at least one GQ eligible in the first-stage sample of the small GQs or the large GQ sampling
frame. The number of GQs selected in this manner is quite small.
Once the final first-phase GQ sample is selected, we assign the selected small GQ facilities and
groups of ten for large GQ facilities to months using a procedure similar to the one used for
sampled HU addresses. We combine and sort all GQ samples from a state by small versus large
stratum and first-phase order of selection.
Consecutive samples are assigned to the 12 months in a pre-determined order, starting with a
randomly determined month.
Because of operational and budgeting constraints, we assign the same month to all sample
groups of ten within certain types of correctional GQs or military barracks. For example, we
assign all samples in federal prisons to September, and data collection may take up to 4.5
months, an exception to the six weeks allowed for all other GQ types. For the samples in non-
federal correctional facilities—state prisons, local jails, halfway houses, military disciplinary
barracks, and other correctional institutions—or military barracks, individual GQ facilities are
randomly assigned to months throughout the year.
Beginning with the 2013 GQ sample, we no longer assign college dorms to the months of May–
August. This is in response to the relatively low interview rates at these GQs during the summer
months. Since 2013, we only assign college dorm GQs to January–April and September–
December.
4.9 Remote Alaska Sample
Remote Alaska is a set of rural areas in Alaska that are difficult to access and for which all HU
addresses are treated as unmailable. In 2022, there were approximately 48,000 HU addresses and
850 GQs in Remote Alaska. Because of the difficulties in field operations during specific months
of the year and the extremely seasonal population in these areas, data collection operations in
Remote Alaska differ from the rest of the country. In both the Period 1 and Period 2 HU address
samples, the month assigned for each Remote Alaska HU address is based on the county, place,
AIANA, or block group (in that order) in which it is contained. We assign all designated
addresses located in each of these geographical entities to either January or July in such a way as
to balance workloads between the months and to keep groups of cases together geographically.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 4: Sample Design and Selection
4-22
We sort the addresses for each month by county and geographical order in the address frame, and
beginning in 2011, all sample addresses are sent directly to CAPI (bypassing mail and internet
for the HU sample) in the appropriate month. We assign the GQ sample in Remote Alaska to
January or July using the same procedure and allow up to six months to complete the HU and
GQ data collection for each of the two data collection periods.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-1
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5.1 Overview
The American Community Survey (ACS) content is designed to meet the needs of federal
government agencies and is a rich source of local area information useful to a wide variety of
data users, including state and local governments, universities, and private businesses. The
Census Bureau coordinates the content development and determination process for the ACS with
the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) through an interagency committee comprised of
more than 30 federal agencies. In addition, the Interagency Council on Statistical Policy
Subcommittee on the ACS (ICSP-SACS) reviews proposals for content changes and provides
input to inform the decisions made by the Chief Statistician of the United States and the Director
of the Census Bureau. All requests for content changes are managed by the ACS Content
Council, whose role is to provide guidance for testing and implementing new content and
changes to existing ACS content. This chapter provides details on the history of content
development for the ACS, survey content, and the content determination process and policy.
5.2 Initial Content Development
The ACS is part of the Decennial Census Program and is an alternative method for collecting the
long-form sample data collected from the 1940 Census to the 2000 Census.35F
36 The long-form
sample historically collected detailed population and housing characteristics once a decade
through questions asked of a sample of the population. Since 2005, the ACS has collected this
detailed information on an ongoing basis, thereby providing more accurate and timely data than
was possible previously. Since 2010, the decennial census only includes a short form that
collects basic information for a total count of the nation’s population.36F
37
Historically, the content of the long form was constrained by including only the questions for
which it was mandatory, required, or programmatic:
• There was a current federal law calling for the use of decennial census data for a
particular federal program (mandatory).
• A federal law (or implementing regulation) clearly required the use of specific data,
and the decennial census was the historical or only source or case law requirements
imposed by the U.S. federal court system needed the data (required).
36 Sampling began in the 1940 Census when a few additional questions were asked of a small sample of people. A
separate long-form questionnaire was not implemented until 1960.
37 In addition to counting each person at every address, the basic information collected on the 2010 Census short form
included a very select set of key demographic characteristics needed for voting rights and other legislative requirements,
including tenure at residence, sex, age, relationship, Hispanic origin, and race.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-2
• The data were necessary for the Census Bureau's operational needs, and there was
no explicit requirement for the use of the data, as explained for mandatory or
required purposes (programmatic).
Constraining the content of the ACS was, and still is, critical because of the mandatory reporting
requirement and to minimize the burden on respondents. To do this, the Census Bureau works
closely with the OMB, the Federal Interagency Committee for the ACS, and the Interagency
Council for Statistical Policy Subcommittee on the ACS (ICSP-SACS).
The Federal Interagency Committee for the ACS was established in July 2000 and includes
representatives from more than 30 federal departments and agencies that use decennial census
data. Working from the 2000 Census long-form justification, the initial focus of the committee
was to verify and confirm legislative justifications for every 2003 ACS question. The members
examined each question and provided for their agency justification(s) by subject matter, the legal
authority for the use, the lowest geographic level required, the variables essential for cross-
tabulation, and the frequency with which the data are needed. They cited the text of statutes and
other legislative documentation and classified their uses of the ACS questions as “mandatory,”
“required,” or “programmatic,” consistent with the constraints of the traditional long form.
In the summer of 2002, the United States (U.S.) Department of Commerce General Counsel’s
Office asked each federal agency’s General Counsel to examine the justifications submitted by
the committee for its agency and, if necessary, to revise the information so that the agency would
be requesting only the most current material necessary to accomplish the statutory departmental
missions in relation to census data. This step ensured that the highest-ranking legal officer in
each agency validated its stated program requirements and data needs.
Only those questions whose subjects were classified as either “mandatory” or “required” were
included on the 2003 ACS questionnaire, along with questions on two programmatic subjects
(fertility and seasonal residence). The result of this review was a 2003 ACS questionnaire with
content almost identical to the 2000 Census long form.
5.3 Initial Content (2003–2007)
From 2003 to 2007, the ACS consisted of 25 housing and 42 population questions (six basic and
36 detailed population questions). (See Table 5-1 for a complete list of ACS topics, listed with
the questionnaire number on the paper questionnaire.) The ACS Group Quarters (GQ)
questionnaire consisted of the same population questions except for the “relationship to
householder” question. The ACS GQ questionnaire also included one housing question: the food
stamp benefit question.

Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-4

The content for the PRCS is identical to that used in the United States (ACS) except for several
questions worded differently to accommodate differences in the data needs and language
differences. Figures 5-1 and 5-2 show examples of two ACS questions modified for the PRCS.
Figure 5-1. Example of ACS Utilities Question Modified for the PRCS

Figure 5-2. Example of ACS Year of Entry Question Modified for the PRCS
ACS Question PRCS Question
5.4 Content Policy and Content Change Process
In 2006, the ACS content development and change process hinged on the status of a question as
mandatory, required, or programmatic, consistent with the constraints of the traditional long
form. These statuses are defined as follows:
• Mandatory – a federal law explicitly calls for the use of decennial census or ACS
data on that question.
• Required – a federal law (or implementing regulation) explicitly requires the use of
data, and the decennial census or the ACS is the historical source; or the data are
needed for case law requirements imposed by the U.S. federal court system.
• Programmatic – the data are needed for program planning, implementation, or
evaluation, and there is no explicit mandate or requirement.
Since 2006, the OMB, in consultation with Congress and the Census Bureau, adopted a more
flexible approach to content determinations for the ACS. In the new content determination
process, the OMB, in consultation with the Census Bureau, considers issues such as frequency of

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-5
data collection, the level of geography needed to meet the required need, and other sources of
data that could meet a requestor’s need in lieu of ACS data. In some cases, legislation still may
be needed to justify including a measure on the ACS. In other cases, OMB may approve a new
measure based on an agency’s justification and program needs.
The Census Bureau recognizes and appreciates the interests of federal partners and stakeholders
in the collection of data for the ACS. Because participation in the ACS is mandatory, the OMB
only approves necessary questions on the ACS. The OMB’s responsibility under the Paperwork
Reduction Act requires that new questions demonstrate the practical utility of the data and that
the respondent burden be minimized (especially for the mandatory ACS collections).
The Census Bureau’s ACS Content Policy is used as a basic guideline for handling all new
question proposals from federal agencies, Congress, and the Census Bureau. The content change
process is part of a risk management strategy to ensure that each new or modified question has
been tested fully and will collect quality data without reducing overall response rates.
One vision for the ACS that emerged in the early years of the ACS program was that of a
national resource to address emerging policy questions in the public sector. The idea that the
ACS could be flexible, providing more frequent opportunities to add new content via new
questions on the ACS itself or through follow-on or supplementary modules, was put forward in
discussions with federal agencies about the program’s future. The response to the survey is
required by law. Therefore, the ACS has generally attained very high participation rates relative
to other federal government surveys. So, the ACS estimates are attractive for users concerned
about statistical bias because of nonparticipation. Also, the continuing efforts to improve the
Census Bureau’s Master Address File that is used for the decennial census, ACS, and other
surveys the Census Bureau conducts make the ACS attractive in terms of its coverage rates.
At the same time, and serving to counterbalance this vision, the OMB and the Census Bureau
recognized the need to develop priorities for including questions on the ACS and ensure that a
consistent set of criteria were used to make decisions about adding or deleting questions from the
ACS, using the ACS as a frame for follow-on surveys, and including a module of questions for a
subsample of ACS cases. This recognition and guidance from the OMB that the respondent
burden for completing the ACS would remain fixed led to the creation of a group tasked with
advising the OMB and Census Bureau on issues related to content practices and policies.38F
39
The OMB and Census Bureau established the ICSP-SACS in 2012.39F
40 The ICSP-SACS exists to
advise the Chief Statistician at OMB and the Director of the Census Bureau consistent with key
governing policies and practices for the ACS from the perspective of the Federal Statistical
39 Respondent burden is measured as the number of minutes each respondent requires to complete the ACS form.
40 For more information see the charter of the Interagency Council on Statistical Policy Subcommittee on the ACS.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-6
System. This subcommittee provides advice on how the ACS can best fulfill its role in the
portfolio of federal household surveys, provide the most useful information with the least amount
of burden, and address technical issues such as the deletion or addition of questions.
To implement a policy for the development of ACS content, the Census Bureau coordinates
input from federal agencies, including the Interagency Committee for the ACS, to ensure broad
input is acquired. The Census Bureau also coordinates the creation of subject-area subcommittee
groups that include representatives from federal agencies and the Census Bureau. These groups
provide expertise in designing question wording and response categories, along with research
and testing objectives and measures, so that the questions will meet the needs of all agencies.
Census Bureau staff review the subcommittee proposals and provide comments and internal
approval of content changes.
The ACS Content Change Process provides guidance for Census Bureau testing for all new or
modified questions before incorporating them into ACS instruments; this guidance is based on
the standards outlined in the Census Bureau’s Statistical Quality Standards, Chapter A2:
Developing Data Collection Instruments and Supporting Materials (U.S. Census Bureau, 2023a).
The Census Bureau will change the content of the ACS only after the OMB gives approval.
Content Change Factors
The OMB and the Census Bureau consider several factors when new content is proposed.
Federal agencies must provide specific information about the new data collection need(s). The
uses of the data must be identified, along with citations to the appropriate statute or regulation, to
determine the appropriateness of collecting it through a mandatory, federal survey. Other federal
surveys or other sources of data are reviewed and considered. The respondent burden must also
be considered.
Federal agencies interested in content changes must be able to demonstrate that they require
detailed data (i.e., for small geographies and/or small populations) with the frequency of ACS
data collection and that failure to obtain the information with this frequency will result in a
failure to meet agency needs. Requests for new ACS content are assessed relative to the impact
on the requesting agency if the data are not collected through the ACS. Federal agencies
requesting new content must demonstrate that they have considered legitimate alternative data
sources and why those alternatives do not meet their needs. Alternative data sources may be used
to develop data products to meet federal agency needs without adding a question to the survey.
Similarly, the Census Bureau may be able to take questions off the survey if it is determined that
alternative data sources could meet the same need as respondent-provided survey answers, as
determined by a thorough research and evaluation process.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-7
Content Change Requirements
Federal agency or Census Bureau proposals for new content or changes to existing ACS
questions are subject to the following requirements:
• ACS content can be added to or revised only once a year. Because the ACS
produces estimates using one year and five years of data, the program strives to
change questions infrequently to ensure questions are consistent over time.
Additionally, revisions are restricted to once per year because of the annual nature
of the survey and the number of operations that must be revised. New content is
incorporated into the ACS only after testing, potentially including a field test, has
been completed and the OMB has provided final approval.
• The requesting federal agency assists with the development of a draft question(s),
works with the Census Bureau and other agencies to revise the question, and
submits the proposal to the OMB and Census Bureau for further review. In addition,
a plan to test new or modified content, including a field test, must be developed in
accordance with the Census Bureau’s Statistical Quality Standards (U.S. Census
Bureau, 2023b).
• Testing is conducted to identify potential measurement error and to determine
whether a change would increase or decrease a respondent’s understanding of what
is being asked. Alternative versions of questions are tested to identify the version
most likely to be answered accurately by respondents, and then are field tested.
5.5 2014 Content Review
In 2014, the Census Bureau conducted a comprehensive content review to ensure that only the
information needed is requested and that the justifications provided by federal agencies for the
ACS questions are current and valid. The results of this analysis informed recommendations
about which questions could be retained in their current form, which could be removed from the
survey, and which could be candidates for further research.
The Census Bureau evaluated every question on the ACS questionnaire as part of this review.
The questions were reviewed against 19 decision criteria developed by the OMB Interagency
Council on Statistical Policy (ICSP) Sub-Committee for the American Community Survey
(ACS). The decision criteria included metrics to assess the benefits associated with the
usefulness of the questions and data and costs associated with respondent burden.
To assess the benefits, information from federal agencies was collected about question and data
usage, such as the level of geographic detail the data are used for and whether the use was
mandatory, required, or programmatic. The Census Bureau also assessed whether a data source
other than ACS could be used to meet the federal agency need and if the data are used as another

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-8
federal survey’s sampling frame. Additionally, coefficients of variation associated with each
question’s county-level estimate and interquartile ranges associated with each question’s median
county-level estimate were calculated.
The Census Bureau analyzed respondent burden by collecting feedback from a survey of field
representatives and examining indicators of burden such as the time taken to answer the
question, item nonresponse, and complaints received.
The information on the benefits and costs was used to identify which questions were candidates
for removal based on being characterized as “Low Benefit and Low Cost” or “Low Benefit and
High Cost.” Questions were candidates for removal if they fell into the Low Benefit/Low Cost or
Low Benefit/High-Cost quadrants and had (1) No Mandatory Uses, (2) No Required Uses at the
block group/tract, and (3) No Required Uses at the place/county/Metropolitan Statistical Area.
After reviewing more than 300 federal agency uses of ACS data and public comment on
proposals, the Business/Medical Office on Property question (housing question 6 on the 2014
ACS) was removed from the ACS beginning in 2016.
For more information on the 2014 Content Review, see Treat, 2014.
5.6 Content Testing
The Census Bureau uses the term “content tests” to describe the testing, research, and evaluation
processes used to determine the best wording, format, and placement of proposed new questions
or revisions to existing questions on the ACS. Content tests are one of several kinds of tests the
Census Bureau conducts as part of the ACS testing program. The primary objective of content
tests is to test whether changes to question wording, response categories, and the redefinition of
underlying constructs could improve the quality of the data collected. In addition to content, the
ACS tests proposed improvements to ACS data collection methods and techniques. Such
improvements include, for example, replacing the initial ACS mailing package with an initial
pressure seal letter, using a new format to organize questions and guidance provided on the ACS
questionnaire, or developing new instructions used by ACS interviewers for the Computer
Assisted Personal Interviewing (CAPI) phase of data collection. This chapter will focus on
content testing alone. Descriptions of other kinds of testing are included in other chapters of this
report.
The Census Bureau conducts a content test for the ACS about every five years. If approved,
changes are usually implemented on the ACS two years after field testing. The Census Bureau
typically conducts cognitive testing to determine the final wording for a field test; field testing is
used to make final recommendations about changes to implement. The methodology used for the
field portion of the content test is designed to be similar to the regular ACS data collection
process. A content test typically has two treatments: a control treatment, which usually has the

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-9
current version of the question, and a test treatment, which has the alternative version of the
question. Data quality may be further evaluated through a follow-up interview with respondents
to either re-ask a question to measure response variance or ask more detailed questions to
measure response bias. Differences between the control and test treatments are evaluated on
several test metrics, typically including gross difference rates, item nonresponse rates, and
measures of distributional changes. Table 5-2 summarizes changes to the ACS content on the
paper questionnaire between 2010 and 2025.40F
41

The following sections describe Content Tests conducted in 2006, 2007, 2010, 2016, and 2022
and the implementation of testing results in the content of the 2008, 2009, 2013, 2019, 2020,

5.7 2006 and 2007 Content Tests
In 2004, planning began for the 2006 ACS Content Test so the Census Bureau could field-test
potential content changes for implementation on the 2008 ACS questionnaire. Consistent with
the procedures described above, the OMB and the Census Bureau asked members of the ACS
Interagency Committee to review the legislative authority for current or proposed ACS
questionnaire content and to identify any questions that needed to be reworded or reformatted.
The 2006 ACS Content Test was the first opportunity to test revisions to the long-form sample
questions used in the 2000 Census.
• The 2006 ACS Content Test included new questions on the subjects of marital
history, health insurance coverage, and veterans’ service-connected disability
ratings. These questions were added to the survey in 2008.
• The 2006 Content Test also tested modifications to questions on 21 other topics.
o Housing topics tested included year built, rooms, bedrooms, plumbing facilities,
kitchen facilities, telephone service, vehicles available, house heating fuel, food
stamp benefits, property value and mortgage status, payments, and real estate
taxes. Based on the results of the test, changes were not made to the heating
fuel, vehicles available, mortgage status, payment, and real estate tax questions.
o Population topics tested included place of birth, citizenship, year of entry,
school enrollment, educational attainment, residence 1-year ago (migration),
disability, military service status, period of military service, employment status,
weeks worked, and industry and occupation. Based on the results of the test,
changes were not made to the place of birth, year of entry, military service
status, period of military service, and industry and occupation topics.
Additional content testing was conducted in the 2007 ACS Content Test on a Field of Degree
question. The National Center for Education Statistics (NCES) began work with the Census
Bureau and two groups of academic researchers in 2006 to develop and test alternative formats
of a Field of Degree question that asked the field of degree for a person having a bachelor’s
degree or higher level of educational attainment. Based on the preliminary research, the Census
Bureau developed and tested two alternative formats of the question in the 2007 ACS Content
42 Content Test Evaluation Reports are available on the Census Bureau’s website.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-12
Test, carried out in the fall of 2007. In 2009, the Census Bureau added a field of degree question
with an open-ended response category to the 2009 ACS.42F
43
The 2006 Content Test and a separate test in 2007 also tested using a sequential format for the
basic demographic questions rather than a grid format for the paper questionnaire (see Chesnut,
2008). Based on those results, the sequential format was adopted in 2008.
See U.S. Census Bureau (2007) for a summary of changes made in the 2008 ACS based on the
results of the 2006 and 2007 Content Tests. Individual topic reports were published in 2007 and
2008 and can be found here: Content Test Evaluation Reports (census.gov).
5.8 2010 Content Test
In response to federal agencies’ requests for new and revised ACS questions, the Census Bureau
conducted the 2010 ACS Content Test.43F
44 The Interagency Committee for the ACS helped
identify possible changes to ACS content and additional new content that would be the subject of
testing. The topics for content testing were new questions on computer ownership and internet
usage and parental place of birth and revisions to questions about veteran status, period of
military service, cash public assistance income, wages, property income, and the Food Stamp
Program name.
• Computer Ownership and Internet Usage – As authorized by the Broadband Data
Improvement Act of 2008, the Federal Communications Commission proposed the
addition of the computer and internet usage topic. The Broadband Data Improvement
Act requires that the Secretary of Commerce, in consultation with the Federal
Communications Commission, expand the ACS to elicit information from residential
households, including those located on native lands, to determine the following:
whether persons at such households own or use computers at their address; whether
persons subscribe to internet service; and, if so, whether they subscribe to dial-up or
broadband internet service at that address. The additions to the questionnaire consist of
three questions with a mix of fixed-choice and open-ended responses.
• Parental Place of Birth – Questions about a person’s mother’s and father’s place of
birth were tested to identify immigration generations (i.e., second generation or
children of immigrants). These generational categories would allow policymakers and
researchers to examine questions about the adaptation and integration of immigrants
and their descendants over time. The results of the 2010 ACS Content Test
demonstrated that questions on parental place of birth were clearly understood by
43 See the ACS questionnaire changes website for details about changes from 2005 to 2009.
44 For full details about the test, please see reports published in 2012 and reports published in 2013 for each topic.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-13
respondents and provided consistent and reliable data. Based on these results. However,
ultimately, the Census Bureau decided not to pursue adding the questions to the ACS.
• Veteran Status – At the request of the Department of Veteran Affairs (VA), the Census
Bureau revised the veteran status question to simplify the question and generate more
reliable and accurate estimates of veterans. The results of the test found that the
estimate of veterans from the test version was comparable to the estimate from the
control version. The VA recommended the adoption of the test version of the veteran
status question.
• Period of Military Service – The VA proposed several revisions to the wording of the
period of military service question to simplify the question and to categorize period of
military service categories in ranges that are meaningful for the VA. The resulting
response distributions and gross difference rates in the test version are not significantly
different from the response distributions and gross difference rates in the control
version in the majority of the categories. The VA recommended the adoption of the
new test version.
• Cash Public Assistance Income – The Census Bureau tested revisions to the cash public
assistance income question to address an underreporting of income of this type.
Researchers believe that the ACS question did not make it clear to the respondents that
they should include participation on behalf of children or that they should report even
single-month participation in a cash public assistance program. Several unfavorable
results of the test were in opposition to the original intent of the question change, and
therefore, it was recommended that changes not be made to the cash public assistance
question.
• Wages – The Census Bureau tested a revision to the wages question in interviewer-
administered modes to improve response by breaking up the question, which references
wages, salary, commissions, bonuses, and tips, into shorter pieces to improve
comprehension. The test found an increase in wage recipiency for the Computer-
Assisted Telephone Interview (CATI) mode. The net difference rates decreased. The
test version's median estimates of wage income were significantly higher than the
control. Item missing data rates for wages amounts and recipiency increased. While the
results were somewhat mixed, it was recommended to implement the test version.
• Property Income – The Census Bureau tested a revision to the property income question
in interviewer-administered modes to improve response by breaking up the question,
which references interest, dividends, net rental income, royalty income, or income from
estates and trusts, into shorter pieces to improve comprehension. Because more
questions were asked in the test version, there was a higher item nonresponse.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-14
However, the test version raised the recipiency rate and net difference rates were
lowered. It was recommended to implement the test version.
• Food Stamp Program Name – At the request of the Food and Nutrition Service, the
Census Bureau revised one housing question on food stamps to incorporate the program
name change to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP). The test did
not identify any differences in response distributions, item nonresponse, or in response
reliability by incorporating the name change and recommended implementing the
change.
Based on results of the 2010 Content Test, one new question topic, computer ownership and
internet usage, was added to the ACS in 2013. In addition, OMB approved the modification of
one housing question and four population questions for the 2013 ACS: veteran status, period of
military service, wages, property income, and the name of the food stamp program.
5.9 2016 Content Test
In 2016, the Census Bureau tested changes to 10 topics on the ACS as well as a new question on
health insurance premiums and subsidies.
Items Tested:
• Telephone Service – To reflect changes in how Americans use the telephone, the
question on telephone service was separated from a list of questions about the
physical characteristics of the housing unit and the instruction to “include calls
using cell phones, land lines, or other phone devices” was added.
• Computer and Internet Use – To keep up with changes in technology, modifications
to questions about computer and internet use were tested and implemented in the
2016 ACS. The test allowed the Census Bureau to evaluate the changes to the
questions.
• Health Insurance Coverage – The health insurance coverage question was tested to
reduce the underestimate of those with Medicaid coverage, allow respondents to
better distinguish direct-purchase insurance from other types, and improve the
accuracy of the estimates.
• Health Insurance Premiums and Subsidies – The Census Bureau also tested a new
question about health insurance premiums and subsidies to measure subsidized
Marketplace coverage (a type of health insurance coverage introduced through the
Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act).
• Commuting Mode – The question on commuting mode was changed to update the
terms used to describe how people get to work. For example, “Streetcar or trolley

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-15
car” was changed to “Light rail, streetcar, or trolley.” The time of departure
question was also revised to address concerns raised among some respondents about
privacy because the question specifically references the time a person leaves home
to go to work.
• Weeks Worked – The question about the number of weeks worked was changed to
an open-ended response format to better meet the needs of data users.
• Class of Worker – Several changes to the questions about the class of workers were
tested to clarify definitions and improve the layout by grouping categories under
general headings (e.g., private sector. Active-duty status in the military was also
added as a response category.
• Industry and Occupation – Changes to the questions on industry and occupation
were tested to make the question easier to understand and obtain the level of detail
needed to classify these data. For example, the space for write-in responses was
increased, and new, consistent examples were provided for the main occupation and
description of job activities or duties.
• Retirement, Survive, and Disability Pensions – New instructions about the types of
retirement, survivor, or disability pensions to include were added to elicit responses
for certain types of income that were not being captured or were only partially
captured, such as 401(k) accounts and Roth Individual Retirement Arrangements
(IRAs).
• Relationship – To reduce errors in reports of same-sex and opposite-sex coupled
households, more detailed response categories were provided for the question on
household relationships and an automated consistency check was introduced in
electronic modes to ask respondents to verify their responses when there are
inconsistencies between sex and relationship.
• Race and Hispanic Origin – A combined version of the questions on race and
Hispanic origin was tested, which included write-in response areas for every major
group and a distinct response option for Middle Eastern or North African (MENA).
A modified version of the separate race and Hispanic origin questions, which
included the option to write in detailed origin information for the White and Black
response categories, was also tested.
Results44F
45
• The test results supported implementing the changes to the questions on telephone
service, commuting mode, time of departure, number of weeks worked, class of

The Census Bureau implemented these changes on the 2019 ACS.
• Given the rapid changes in technology, the Census Bureau chose to implement
changes to the computer and internet use questions on the ACS in 2016 in
conjunction with field testing. The test results further validated the decision to
revise the computer and internet use questions.
• The test results showed that the changes to the health insurance coverage question
did not appreciably improve the health insurance coverage estimates and did not
support implementing these changes. The lessons learned in the 2016 test informed
future testing of the coverage question. The test results did support implementing
the new question on health insurance premiums and subsidies, which were added to
the ACS in 2019.
• The Census Bureau implemented the version of the race and Hispanic origin
questions used on the 2020 Census on the ACS in 2020.45F

5.10 2022 Content Test
The following topics were included in the 2022 Content Test: household roster, educational
attainment, health insurance coverage, disability, income, SNAP, and labor force questions.
Additionally, three new questions were tested on solar panels, electric vehicles, and sewage
disposal. A summary of changes tested for each topic is as follows:
• Household Roster – The roster instructions have not changed since the 1990s while
household living arrangements have increased in complexity. The revisions
attempted to capture more complex living situations and improve within household
coverage, especially among young children and tenuously attached residents.
• Educational Attainment – A relatively high percentage of respondents are selecting
the response category, “No schooling completed.” Ongoing research suggests that
this includes adults who have completed some level of schooling. The revision
attempted to reduce the erroneous reports in this category through formatting and
wording changes to clarify the response options.
• Health Insurance Coverage – The purpose of testing the revised health insurance
coverage question was to enhance question reliability and validity. Since
implementation in 2008, research has found that Medicaid and other means-tested
programs were underreported in the ACS and that direct-purchase coverage was
overreported, in part because of misreporting of non-comprehensive health plans
46 The version of the race and Hispanic origin questions was not the version tested in the 2016 ACS Content Test.
Results from the 2015 decennial Content Test ultimately led to the content decision.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-17
and reporting multiple coverage types for the same plan. Moreover, revisions to the
health insurance coverage question would help capture changes to the health
insurance landscape that occurred with and since the passage of the Patient
Protection and Affordable Care Act.
Changes to the health insurance coverage question included the following:
reordering some response options and rewording response options for direct
purchase, Medicaid, employer, and veteran’s health care. A second version of the
question tested these same changes along with a change to the format of the
question that added an explicit response category for those who were uninsured.
• Disability – The purpose of testing revisions to the series of six disability questions
was to capture population information on functioning in a manner that reflected
advances in the measurement of disability and was conceptually consistent with the
World Health Organization’s International Classification of Functioning, Disability,
and Health (ICF) disability framework. Changes included using graded response
categories to reflect the continuum of functional abilities (the control questions use
a dichotomous yes/no response), reordering the questions, and modifying question
text. Additionally, a new test question attempted to capture difficulties related to
psychosocial and cognitive disability in addition to problems with speech.
• Income and SNAP – The Census Bureau conducted research to determine the
feasibility of using administrative sources as a replacement or supplement to the
income questions currently fielded on the survey. Administrative data sources on
employment, income, and public assistance benefits from the Internal Revenue
Service, Social Security Administration, and state administrative offices could meet
the agency needs for many types of income, transfer benefits, and employment data.
If administrative data use in ACS production was implemented, it could provide far-
reaching benefits for multiple ACS topics including income, SNAP, and
employment.
To better align with administrative data sources on many types of income and
transfer benefits, the Census Bureau tested a change in the reference period from the
“past 12 months” to asking about the prior calendar year. Changes to the income
and SNAP questions, aside from the reference period change, included the
following: updating overall instructions along with instructions for public assistance
and retirement income, changing question wording for self-employment, public
assistance, and total income, and collecting rental income separately from the
interest question. Two versions of the income questions were tested: one with a
change in reference period and the question modifications and the other with only
question modifications. The SNAP question only tested changes to the reference
period; the question itself did not change.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-18
• Labor Force Questions – To align with the income question series, the Census
Bureau tested a change in the reference period from “during the past 12 months” to
asking about the prior calendar year. Changing the reference period to the prior
calendar year would allow the ACS to better align with administrative records,
which, if used, could improve the quality of ACS estimates. Additional changes to
the labor force question series included the following: adding a question for
respondents who have worked in the past five years (to implement the change in
reference period) and modifying question instructions (tested in two formats).
• Solar Panels – This new question was tested to determine if the housing unit uses
solar panels that generate electricity. Adding this question would provide data on
operational solar panels on a housing unit level across the country. This information
would help the Energy Information Administration (EIA) match energy
consumption to energy production across the United States.
• Electric Vehicles – This new question was tested to determine if there are plug-in
electric vehicles kept at the housing unit. Adding this question would provide the
ability to project future energy sources, infrastructure, and consumer needs for the
growing popularity of electric vehicles. The ACS is the only data source at the
housing unit level for adequately making these projections.
• Sewage Disposal – This new question was tested to determine if the housing unit is
connected to a public sewer, septic tank, or other type of sewage system. Adding
this question would provide consistent data on the status of decentralized
wastewater infrastructure in rural and other communities. These data are needed to
protect public health and water quality and to understand and meet the country's
growing infrastructure needs. The ACS is the only available survey that can provide
these levels of data in a timely, consistent, and standardized manner.
The 2022 Content Test included a control and two experimental treatments to evaluate the
proposed changes. The Control Treatment included the current ACS (production) version of the
questions. New topics were also included in this treatment. The Test Treatment included the test
question version for all topics except the household roster. Because changes to within-household
coverage can impact the results of person-level questions, the household roster question(s) were
the same as the Control Treatment to avoid confounding. Changes to the roster question(s) were
tested in a separate treatment, called the Roster Test Treatment. Additionally, health insurance
coverage, labor force, and income included a second set of experimental questions in the Roster
Test Treatment.
Each topic was evaluated using a variety of metrics, including item missing data rates, response
distributions, comparisons to benchmarks and administrative data, response reliability, and other
topic-specific metrics. Comparisons were made between the Control Treatment and the
experimental treatments. In some cases, the two experimental treatments were also compared.

Results46F
47
• The test results supported implementing the changes to the questions on household
roster, educational attainment, health insurance coverage, and labor force (wording
changes only) along with the addition of three new questions on solar panels,
electric vehicles, and sewage disposal. The Census Bureau will implement these
changes on the 2025 ACS.
o Reference period changes to SNAP, income, and labor force questions are
pending further research and implementation of administrative data use.
Question changes to the income question are also pending further research.
o The Census Bureau initially proposed to change the disability question as a
result of the 2022 Content Test. However, based on public feedback on the
proposal, the Census Bureau decided to retain the current ACS disability
questions for the 2025 ACS and not move forward with the change (Santos,
2024).
• In addition to the topics included in the 2022 Content Test, three additional topics
had changes that were cognitively tested: home heating fuel, condominium fees, and
transportation to work. The Census Bureau, with input from the ICSP-SACS and
approval from OMB, implemented changes to these questions on the 2024 ACS
based on the cognitive testing results.
o Home Heating Fuel – The change to the home heating fuel question updated the
wording of the natural gas and bottled gas categories to aid respondents in
identifying the correct category more easily by using more commonly used
terminology. In Puerto Rico, the question wording also changed to indicate
respondents should only include fuel that heats their homes.
o Condominium and HOA Fees – The condominium fees question was extended
to include homeowners’ association (HOA) fees. Data sources continue to show
housing units that are part of HOAs outnumber housing units in condominiums.
In order to provide more comprehensive and accurate costs of owning a home,
the ACS needs to capture HOA fees for these homes. Adding these fees to the
existing condominium fees question avoids adding a new question to the ACS
and, therefore, minimizes respondent burden.
o Transportation to Work – The transportation to work question was updated to
include ride-sharing services as a mode of transportation to work to account for
new and growing travel trends. The current category, “taxicab,” was updated to
“taxi or ride-sharing services.” This will reduce ambiguity in the current
47 For full details about the test, please see reports published in 2022 and reports published in 2023 for each topic.

Chapter 5: Content Development Process
5-20
question about where respondents should report ride-sharing commutes and will
allow the government to monitor changes in transportation patterns for planning
purposes.
• Period of Military Service – A change was made to the Period of Military Service
question based on feedback from the VA. The VA requested that the ACS adjust the
dates for the Vietnam War and Korean War to reflect the dates that they use for
program evaluation (each period was adjusted by one month). The VA also
requested that “Post 9/11” be added as a descriptor for the current service period,
“Vietnam era” be changed to “Vietnam War,” and names of war periods be moved
to the end of the date range for a uniform appearance. The VA requested that the
date ranges use the word “through” instead of “to” for clarity. The updated dates for
period of service match the dates that the VA uses for program evaluation as well as
the official historical dates of war periods published by the Congressional Research
Service. Moving names of war periods to the end of service categories creates a
more uniform appearance of the question text, with dates listed first for all periods.
These changes were recommended and approved for implementation on the 2024
ACS without cognitive or field testing.
