Calculation of Final Housing Unit Factors
Before the calculation of person weights, each HU has a single weight which is independent of
the characteristics of the persons residing in the HU. After the calculation of person weights, a
new HU weight is computed that takes into account the characteristics of the householder in the
HU. In each interviewed occupied HU, the householder is identified as one of the persons who
rents or owns the HU. Adjustment of the HU weight to account for the householder
characteristics is done by assigning a householder factor (HHF) for an HU equal to the person
post-stratification factor (PPSF) of the householder. Their PPSFs give an indication of relative
coverage for households whose householders have the same demographic characteristics. The

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 3.0) – Chapter 11: Weighting and Estimation
11-24
HHF adjustment uses this information to adjust for the resultant bias. Note that because the total
weight for occupied units was constrained in the person raking methodology, this adjustment
impacts the distribution of the weight of the housing unit weights to reflect the relative coverage
rates of different householders, but it generally does not perform any further adjustments for
overall coverage. The adjustment for overall coverage for all housing units was addressed in the
HPF adjustment. Vacant HUs are given an HHF of 1.0 because they have no householders.
The adjusted HU weight accounting for householder characteristics is computed as a
multiplication of the adjusted HU weight after the HU post-stratification factor adjustment
(WHPF) with the householder factor (HHF). Therefore, 𝐵𝐵𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐺𝐺 = 𝐵𝐵𝐻𝐻𝑃𝑃𝐺𝐺 × 𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐺𝐺, where WHHF
is the adjusted HU weight after the householder factor adjustment. The HU weight after the
householder factor adjustment becomes the final HU weight.
The ACS weighting procedure results in two separate sets of weights: one for HUs and one for
persons residing within HUs. However, since the housing unit weight is equal to the person
weight of the householder, the survey produces logically consistent estimates of occupied
housing units, households, and householders. With this weighting procedure, the survey estimate
of total HUs differs slightly from the PEP total housing unit estimates but is typically within a
tenth of a percent at the county level.
11.11 Multiyear Estimation Methodology
The multiyear estimation methodology involves reweighting the data for each sample address in
the 5-year period and is not just a simple average of the 1-year estimates. The weighting
methodology for the multiyear estimation is very similar to the methodology used for the single-
year weighting. Thus, only the differences between the single- and multiyear weighting are
described in this section.
Pooling the Data
The data for all sample addresses over the multiyear period are pooled together into one file. The
single-year base weights are then adjusted by the reciprocal of the number of years in the period
so that each year contributes its proportional share to the multiyear estimates. For the 5-year
weighting, the base weights are thus all divided by five.
The interview month assigned to each address is also recoded so that all the data from the entire
period appears as though it came from a one-year period. For example, in the 2018–2022 5-year
weighting, all addresses that were originally assigned an interview month of January 2018, 2019,
2020, 2021, or 2022 are assigned the common interview month of January. Thus, when the
weighting is performed, those records are all treated as though they come from the same month
for the VMS adjustment. This should better preserve the seasonal trends that may be present in
the population as captured by the ACS.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 3.0) – Chapter 11: Weighting and Estimation
11-25
Geography
The geography for all sample addresses in the period is updated into the common geography of
the final year. This allows the data to be tabulated in a consistent, constant geography that is the
most recent and likely most relevant to data users. When tabulating estimates for an area, all
interviews from the period that are considered to be inside the boundaries of that area in the final
year of the period are included in the estimates regardless of if they were considered to be inside
the boundaries for that area at the time of interview. As a by-product of this methodology, the
ACS is also able to publish multiyear estimates for newly created places or counties that did not
exist when the interviews for the addresses in that place or county were collected.
Derivation of the Multiyear Controls
Since the multiyear estimate is an estimate for the period, the controls are not those of a
particular year but are the average of the annual independent population estimates over the
period. The Population Estimates Program refreshes its entire time series of estimates going back
to the previous census each year using the most current data and methodology. Each of these
time series is considered a “vintage.” To use the best available population estimates as controls,
the ACS multiyear weighting uses the population estimates of the most recent vintage for all
years in the period to derive the multiyear controls.
These derived estimates are created for the housing unit, group quarters population, and total
population for use as controls in the multiyear weighting. The derived county-level housing unit
estimates are the simple average across all years in the period. Since the average is typically not
an integer, the result is rounded to form the final estimate. Likewise, the derived group quarters
population estimates for state by major type group are the simple average across all years in the
period. Those averages are then control rounded so that the rounded state average estimate is
within 1 of the unrounded estimate. Finally, the derived total population estimates by race,
ethnicity, age, and sex are averaged across all years in the period and control rounded to form the
final derived estimates. This is done before the collapsing of the estimates into the 156 cells per
weighting area needed for the demographic dimension of the household person weighting as
described in the single-year person weighting section.
The weighting areas used for the multiyear estimation are generally smaller than those used for
the single-year estimation. They are still formed by complete counties or aggregations of
counties, and they must meet a threshold of 400 unweighted person interviews at the time of
their formation. In addition, for the five-year estimation, the weighting area must have a
minimum population of 2,500.
However, since there is no publication threshold for the five-year data product, there are counties
that are not their own weighting area, and therefore, greater differences between the ACS and
PEP estimates of total population may exist, particularly for counties smaller than 2,500 in total

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 3.0) – Chapter 11: Weighting and Estimation
11-26
population. For the formation of the subcounty control areas, the single-year threshold of 24,000
in total population is reduced for the five-year weighting to 2,500.
Model-assisted Estimation
Once the data are pooled and put into the geography of the final year, they are weighted using
the single-year weighting methodology through the NIF adjustment. It is after this adjustment
that the only multiyear-specific weighting step is implemented, the model-assisted estimation
procedure. An earlier research project (Starsinic, 2005) compared the variances of ACS tract-
level estimates formed from the 1999–2001 ACS to the variances of the 2000 Census long-form
estimates. The results of that research showed that the variances of the ACS tract-level estimates
were higher in relation to the long form than was expected based on sample size alone. The
primary source of that increased variance was attributed to the lack of ACS subcounty controls at
the tract-level or lower as was used for the long form.
Several options were explored on how the ACS might improve our estimates of variance for
subcounty estimates. One option considered was to use the ACS sampling frame counts as
subcounty controls. Other options explored ways to create subcounty population controls,
including tract-level population controls. The final approach that was chosen introduces a model-
assisted estimation step into the multiyear weighting that makes use of both the sampling frame
counts and administrative records to reduce the level of variance in the subcounty estimates (Fay,
2006). An important feature of the model-assisted estimation procedure is that the administrative
record data are not used directly to produce ACS estimates. The administrative record data are
only used to help reduce the level of variance. This is an important property of this step as the
coverage for administrative records for small areas such as tracts can vary significantly year-to-
year. Had we pursued using the administrative record directly to impact the estimates, this
variability in coverage could have led to unpredictable impacts on the quality of the ACS data.
By using the administrative record data for variance reduction only, the impact of this coverage
on the ACS is principally on the degree of variance reduction that is achieved and not on the
directly on estimates themselves. The published ACS estimates are still formed from weighted
totals of the ACS survey data.
The model-assisted estimation step is calculated at the at the tract level for the ACS 5-year data.
The entire model-assisted estimation process is summarized in these steps.
1. Create frame counts for tracts that contain at least 300 housing unit addresses.
2. Link the administrative records to the ACS sampling frame (the Master Address
File or MAF) and drop administrative records that cannot be linked.
3. Form unweighted geographic totals of the linked administrative record
characteristics.
4. Apply the WNIF weights at the housing-unit level to the linked administrative
records that fall into the ACS sample. The weighted estimates at this step
represent (essentially) unbiased estimates of the unweighted totals in Step 3.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 3.0) – Chapter 11: Weighting and Estimation
11-27
5. Using generalized regression estimation, fit a model to calibrate the ACS
weights so that the weighted totals from the linked ACS records match the
unweighted totals from Step 3 and so that the weighted ACS estimate of HUs
match the frame totals in Step 1. The categories of the variables considered in
the regression are collapsed or removed as necessary to fit a good model.
6. Proceed with the remaining steps of the ACS weighting starting with the HPF
adjustments, including the person weighting using the derived multiyear
controls as described in the preceding section.
Frame Counts: The base weights (BW), which reflect the sampling probabilities of selection,
should sum to the count of records on the sampling frame at the county and, generally, the
subcounty level. However, after the weighting steps that follow the base weight assignment
through the noninterview adjustment, the weighted subcounty distribution of the interviewed
sample cases can deviate from the original frame distribution. This can impact both the
subcounty estimates and the variances on those estimates. The use of the frame counts
reestablishes the original subcounty distribution of housing unit addresses on the frame in the
weighted sample. This control to the frame counts is the simplest model and is used if a model
with administrative record data cannot be estimated. Otherwise, it is one part of the entire
calibration performed in this step.
Link Administrative Records to Frame: The administrative record data used for this step is
created from linking two primary files maintained by the Economic Reimbursable Division at the
Census Bureau. The first file includes person characteristics and has been created from a
combination of Social Security and census information. The second file uses administrative
records to identify all possible addresses of the persons on the first file. A merged file is then
created which contains only the age, sex, race, and Hispanic origin of each person and an
identifier that links that person to the best address available in the MAF via a Master Address
File ID (MAFID). No other characteristics or publicly identifiable information are present on the
file. This file is updated annually to account for new births, death information, and for updated
address information.
Administrative Universe Counts: For each MAFID, it is possible to create household
demographic totals of people by age/sex and race/ethnicity from the merged administrative
records for each address that is matched to the MAF. The age/sex totals are calculated within
seven categories:
1. All persons ages 0–17
2. All persons ages 18–29
3. Males ages 30–44
4. Females ages 30–44
5. Males ages 45–64

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 3.0) – Chapter 11: Weighting and Estimation
11-28
6. Females ages 45–64
7. All persons ages 65 and older
The race/ethnicity totals are calculated within four categories:
1. All Hispanics regardless of race
2. All non-Hispanic blacks
3. All non-Hispanic whites
4. All non-Hispanics other races
These household-level totals can then be used to create unweighted tract-, place- and MCD-level
administrative record universe totals using the geography associated with the address.
Weighted Administrative Sample Counts: The administrative records that match to the
sampling frame can also be linked to the actual ACS sample records themselves. Using the
WNIF weights, the records that match to the ACS sample can then be used to create weighted
administrative record totals for the same geographic areas. Since the ACS sample weights should
reflect the frame counts, these weighted administrative record totals should be an unbiased
estimate of the unweighted matched universe totals.
Applying GREG Estimation: Using generalized regression estimation (or GREG), the ACS
weights are first calibrated so that the weighted administrative record totals match the
unweighted universe counts for the seven age/sex categories. Two conditions are checked: is the
regression equation solvable and are all of the resulting weights greater than 0.5. If either
condition fails, then the age/sex categories are collapsed and the regression is attempted again.
Two levels of collapsing are attempted:
Collapsing across age/sex categories into three categories: all persons ages 0–17, all
persons ages 18–44, and all persons 45 and older.
Collapse all categories into a single cell of total administrative persons.
If the condition still fails after the second level of collapsing, then the administrative record data
are not used.
If the regression passes using at least the single cell of total administrative persons, then an
attempt is made to add the race/ethnicity covariates to the model. First, a collapsing procedure is
run that tests which race/ethnicity categories can be used. The criteria for including a
race/ethnicity category in the regression is that both the administrative records universe count for
the category being tested and the total for all other categories must be greater than 300 persons.
This procedure is carried out first for the largest race/ethnicity category not including the non-
Hispanic white category, then the next largest such category, and finally the last remaining
category other than non-Hispanic white.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 3.0) – Chapter 11: Weighting and Estimation
11-29
As an example, if the largest category other than non-Hispanic white was the Hispanic category,
then the first test would be if 1) the Hispanic category had a universe count which was greater
than 300 and 2) the other three categories combined had a universe count greater than 300. If it
passes, the Hispanic category is flagged to be included and the remaining categories are tested. If
the next largest category is non-Hispanic black, it is tested to determine if its universe count is
greater than 300 and if the balance, now only the non-Hispanic other races and non-Hispanic
white, is greater than 300. If it passes, then the procedure moves on to test the smallest category
other than non-Hispanic white. In this example, that is the non-Hispanic other race category. If a
similar test on that category fails (or on any previous attempt) then the race collapsing is
complete and the covariates for each race/ethnicity category that passed are added to the model.
The regression is then attempted including both the age/sex and race/ethnicity covariates. The
same conditions used in the age/sex category collapsing are applied to the new attempt. If the
regression passes both conditions, then the covariate matrix is considered final. If the regression
fails either condition, then the smallest race/ethnicity category is not included in the model and
the regression is attempted again. This process continues until either the regression passes or all
race/ethnicity covariates have been removed. The final result of this step is the creation of the
GREG Weighting Factor (GWTF) for each ACS record, which captures the calibration
performed in the regression. A summary of the impact of the GWTF is given in Table 11-8.

After this step is complete, the multiyear weighting mirrors the single-year weighting, picking up

Other Multiyear Estimation Steps
In addition to the adjustments to the single-year weighting methodology for weighting the
multiyear data, there are other steps involved in the multiyear estimation that are not weighting
related. These include standardizing definitions of variables, updating the geography for place of
work and migration characteristics, and the adjustment of income, value, and other dollar
amounts for inflation over the period. The details of these adjustments are given in Chapter 10.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 12: Variance Estimation
12-1
Chapter 12: Variance Estimation
12.1 Overview
Sampling error is the uncertainty associated with an estimate based on data gathered from a
sample of the population rather than the full population. Note that sample-based estimates will
vary depending on the particular sample selected from the population. Measures of the
magnitude of sampling error, such as the variance and the standard error (the square root of the
variance), reflect the variation in the estimates over all possible samples that could have been
selected from the population using the same sampling methodology.
The American Community Survey (ACS) is committed to providing its users with measures of
sampling error along with each published estimate. To accomplish this, all published ACS
estimates are accompanied by 90 percent margins of error based on ACS direct variance
estimates. Due to the complexity of the sampling design and the weighting adjustments
performed on the ACS sample, unbiased design-based variance estimators do not exist. As a
consequence, the direct variance estimates are computed using a replication method that repeats
the estimation procedures independently several times. The variance of the full sample is then
estimated using the variability across the resulting replicate estimates. Note that the methodology
for variance estimation used for the ACS has been relatively stable over its history. For
reference, this chapter reflects the methodology used for the 2022 data products released in 2023.
Although the variance estimates calculated using this procedure are not completely unbiased, the
current method produces variances that are accurate enough for analysis of the ACS data.
For Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS) data users, replicate weights are provided to
approximate standard errors for the PUMS-tabulated estimates. Design factors are also provided
with the PUMS data, so PUMS data users can compute standard errors of their statistics using
either the replication method or the design factor method.
12.2 Variance Estimation for Housing Unit and Person Estimates
Unbiased estimates of variances for ACS estimates do not exist because of the systematic sample
design, as well as the ratio adjustments used in estimation. As an alternative, ACS implements a
replication method for variance estimation. An advantage of this method is that the variance
estimates can be computed without consideration of the form of the statistics or the complexity
of the sampling or weighting procedures, such as those being used by the ACS.
The ACS employs the Successive Differences Replication (SDR) method (Wolter, 1984; Fay &
Train, 1995; Judkins, 1990) to produce variance estimates. It has been the method used to
calculate ACS estimates of variances since the start of the survey. The SDR was designed to be
used with systematic samples for which the sort order of the sample is informative, as in the case

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 12: Variance Estimation
12-2
of the ACS’s geographic sort. Applications of this method were developed to produce estimates
of variances for the Current Population Survey (U.S. Census Bureau, 2006) and the Census 2000
Long Form estimates (Gbur & Fairchild, 2002).
In the SDR method, the first step in creating a variance estimate is constructing the replicate
factors. Replicate base weights are then calculated by multiplying the base weight for each
housing unit (HU) by the factors. The weighting process is then rerun, using each set of replicate
base weights in turn, to create final replicate weights. Replicate estimates are created by using
the same estimation method as the original estimate but applying each set of replicate weights
instead of the original weights. Finally, the replicate and original estimates are used to compute
the variance estimate based on the variability between the replicate estimates and the full sample
estimate.
The following steps produce the ACS direct variance estimates:
1. Compute replicate factors.
2. Compute replicate weights.
3. Compute variance estimates.
Replicate Factors
Computation of replicate factors begins with selecting a Hadamard matrix of order R (a multiple
of 4), where R is the number of replicates. A Hadamard matrix H is a k-by-k matrix with all
entries either 1 or −1, such that H'H = kI (that is, the columns are orthogonal). For the ACS, the
number of replicates is 80 (R = 80). Each of the 80 columns represents one replicate.
Next, a pair of rows in the Hadamard matrix is assigned to each sampling cluster. (For the HU
sample, this is the HU; for the group quarters sample, it is the “hit.”60F
61 ) An algorithm assigns two
rows of an 80×80 Hadamard matrix to each cluster. The ACS uses a repeating sequence of 780
pairs of rows in the Hadamard matrix to assign rows to each record in a particular sort order
(Navarro, 2001a). The assignment of Hadamard matrix rows repeats every 780 records until all
records receive a pair of rows from the Hadamard matrix. The first row of the matrix, in which
every cell is always equal to one, is not used.
The replicate factor for each record is then determined from these two rows of the 80×80
Hadamard matrix. For record i (i = 1,…,n, where n is the sample size) and replicate r (r =
1,…,80), the replicate factor is computed as:

The expectation is that 50 percent of replicate factors will be 1, and the other 50 percent will be
evenly split between 1.7 and 0.3 (Gunlicks, 1996).

Replicate Weights
Replicate weights are produced in a way similar to that used to produce full sample final
weights. All of the weighting adjustment processes performed on the full sample final survey
weights (such as applying noninterview adjustments and population controls) also are carried out
for each replicate weight. However, collapsing patterns are retained from the full sample
weighting and are not determined again for each set of replicate weights.
Before applying the weighting steps explained in Chapter 11, the replicate base weight (RBW)
for replicate r is computed by multiplying the full sample base weight (BW— see Chapter 11 for
the computation of this weight) by the replicate factor ƒ i,r; that is, RBWi,r = BWi × ƒi,r, where
RBWi,r is the replicate base weight for the i-th HU and the r-th replicate (r = 1, …, 80).
One can elaborate on the previous example of the replicate construction using five cases and four
replicates: Suppose the full sample BW values are given under the second column of the
following table (Table 12-2). Then, the replicate base weight values are given in columns 7−10.

and proceeding to the population control adjustment or raking). Basically, the weighting

(as in Chapter 11).
By the end of this process, 80 final replicate weights for each HU and person record are
produced.
Variance Estimates
Given the replicate weights, the computation of variance for any ACS estimate is
straightforward. Suppose that θ is an ACS estimate of any type of statistic, such as mean, total,
or proportion. Let θ0 denote the estimate computed based on the full sample weight, and θ1 , θ2 ,
…, θ80 denote the estimates computed based on the replicate weights. The variance of θ0 , v(θ0),

and the full sample estimate θ0 . The formula is as foll ows61 F

This equation holds for count estimates as well as any other types of estimates, including
percentages, ratios, and medians.
Special Cases for Variance Estimates—Controlled Estimates
There are certain cases, however, where this formula does not apply. The first and most
important case is for estimates that are “controlled” to population totals and have their standard
errors set to zero. These are estimates that are forced to equal independent population estimates
during the weighting process’s raking step, for example, total population and collapsed age, sex,
and Hispanic origin estimates for weighting areas. Although race is included in the raking
procedure, race group estimates are not controlled; the categories used in the weighting process
(see Chapter 11) do not match the published tabulation groups because of multiple race
responses and the “Some Other Race” category. Information on the final collapsing of the person
post-stratification cells is passed from the weighting to the variance estimation process in order
to identify estimates that are controlled. This identification is done independently for all
weighting areas and then is applied to the geographic areas used for tabulation. Standard errors
for those estimates are set to zero, and published margins of error are set to “*****” (with an
appropriate accompanying footnote).
Special Cases for Variance Estimates—Zero Count Estimates
Another special case deals with zero-estimated counts of people, households, or HUs. A direct
application of the replicate variance formula leads to an estimated standard error of zero for a
zero-estimated count. However, there may be people, households, or HUs with that characteristic
in that area that were not selected to be in the ACS sample, but a different sample might have
selected them, so an estimated standard error of zero is inaccurate. For these cases, a model-
based estimation of standard error was implemented.

census year, the ACS zero-estimated counts (for characteristics included in the 100 percent

Special Cases for Variance Estimates—Zero Percent Estimates
Finally, a method must be used to create an approximate standard error for both ACS zero
percent and 100 percent estimates. In both cases, as with the zero counts above, all replicates are
equal, and the replicate formula produces a zero standard error. To preserve the desirable
property SE(p) = SE(1–p), we want to use the same approximation for zero percent and 100
percent, and we will present the zero percent case for this discussion. (Note that we do not
produce approximate standard errors for other zero estimates, such as ratios or medians.)
Let Z be the unweighted count of respondents with a given characteristic. If Z is modeled as a
Poisson variable with parameter λ, what is a likely value for λ if Z = 0? We could find the value
λ0 such that the probability of λ ≤ λ0 is 0.9, given Z = 0 (sort of a 90% upper confidence bound).
Z can also be represented as X / AW, where X is the weighted estimate of the numerator, and
AW is the average weight, as described in the previous section.
Upper Bound (E(Z)) = Upper Bound (E(X / AW)) = 2.3
⇒ Upper Bound (E(X)) = 2.3 × AW
⇒ Upper Bound (E(X/Y)) = 2.3 × AW / Y
This gives an “upper bound” value for the proportion that we can use to calculate a
corresponding “upper bound” value for its standard error.
To be conservative, if R > 0.5 (which will only happen if Y is fairly small), then 0.5 can be used
instead of R, since SE(R) is a maximum for a given AW and Y when R = 0.5.

This is because while the ACS samples areas at up to 10 percent of the housing units per year,

where
Generically, n is the unweighted sample size, and N is the unweighted universe size. The ACS
uses two separate FPC factors: one for HUs responding by mail or internet, and a second for HUs
responding via personal visit follow-up.
The FPC is typically applied as a multiplicative factor “outside” the variance formula. However,
under certain simplifying assumptions, the variance using the replicate factors after applying the
FPC factor is equal to the original variance multiplied by the FPC factor. This method allows a
direct application of the FPC to each housing unit’s or person’s set of replicate weights and a
seamless incorporation into the current variance production methodology rather than having to
keep track of multiplicative factors when tabulating across areas of different sampling rates.
The adjusted replicate factors are used to create replicate base weights, and ultimately final
replicate weights. It is expected that the improvement in the variance estimate will carry through
the weighting and will be seen when the final weights are used.
The ACS FPC factor could be applied at any geographic level. Since the ACS sampling rates are
determined at the small area level (mainly census tracts and governmental units), a low level of
geography was desirable. At higher levels, the high sampling rates in specific blocks would
likely be masked by the lower rates in surrounding blocks. For that reason, the factors are applied
at the census tract level.
Group quarters persons do not have an FPC factor applied to their replicate factors.

incorporated into processing (see the description in Chapter 11). Group Quarters (GQ) person
records were imputed into not-in-sample GQs in order to improve estimates for the
characteristics of the GQ population for substate areas. While the ACS does not publish GQ

The number of these wholly imputed person records has generally ranged between 80 percent
and 90 percent of the number of interviewed GQ persons, so estimates based on this
methodology could contain a sizable amount of imputation error. This imputation error would
not be picked up by the normal replicate variance formula, leading to an underestimate of
standard errors.
An adjustment was needed to the production replicate weight variance methodology to account
for the non-negligible amount of additional variation being introduced by the new technique.
However, it needed to be simple enough to be implemented within the structure of the current
estimate and variance tabulation system. Creating a set of design factors to inflate the basic
replicate variance was determined to be the best approach.
To that end, 40 separate sets of imputed GQ data were created, with the full ACS weighting
applied to each set. Estimates selected from ACS subject table S2601A, “Characteristics of the
Group Quarters Population in the United States,” were created from each of the 40 sets at the
state and county level. The total variance of the estimates, including from the imputation, was
calculated using a formula from Rubin (1996):
where the first term is the average “within imputation” variance (the standard ACS variance),
and the second term is the “between imputation” variance that is not captured by the standard
method.
Preliminary design factors were created by estimate characteristic group, major GQ type, and
state by fitting a no-intercept regression model with the independent variable as the “within
imputation” variance and the dependent variable as the total variance in the above formula.
Variability in the preliminary design factors was much more pronounced between major GQ
types than between characteristics, so (conservative) “overall” design factors were calculated to
create a single design factor for each state by major GQ type.
These design factors were applied to the replicate factors, similarly to the multiyear finite
population correction factor described above, although with the intention of increasing the
variance rather than decreasing it.

12.3 Margin of Error and Confidence Interval
Once the standard errors have been computed, margins of error (MOE) are produced for each
estimate. These are the measures of overall sampling error presented along with each published
ACS estimate. All published ACS margins of error presented in the ACS data products are based
on a 90 percent confidence level, which is the Census Bureau’s standard (U.S. Census Bureau,
2023b). A margin of error contains two components: the standard error of the estimate and a
multiplication factor based on a chosen confidence level. For the 90 percent confidence level, the
value of the multiplication factor used by the ACS is 1.645.
The margin of error of an estimate θ can be computed as:
Margin of error(
θ ) = 1.645 × 𝑆𝑆𝐴𝐴(
θ )
where SE(θ) is the standard error of the estimate θ. Given thi s margin of error, the 90 percent
confidence interval can be computed as:
θ ± Margin of error(
θ )
That i s, the lower bound of the confidence interval i s [θ − margin of error (θ )], and the upper
bound of the confidence interval i s [θ + margin of error (θ )]. Roughly speaking, thi s interval i s a
range that will contain the ‘‘full population value’’ of the estimated characteristic with a known
probability.
Users are cautioned to consider ‘‘logical’’ boundaries when creating confidence bounds from the
margins of error. For example, a small population estimate may have a calculated lower bound of
less than zero. A negative number of people does not make sense, so the lower bound should be
set to zero instead. Likewise, bounds for percentages should not go below zero percent or above
100 percent. For other characteristics, like income, negative values may be legitimate.
Given the confidence bounds, a margin of error can be computed as the difference between an
estimate and its upper or lower confidence bounds:
Margin of error = max(upper bound − estimate, estimate − lower bound)

That determination is made by initially calculating:
If Z < −1.645 or Z > 1.645, the difference between the estimates is significant at the 90 percent
level. Determinations of statistical significance are made using unrounded values of the standard
errors, so users may not be able to achieve the same result using the standard errors derived from
the rounded estimates and margins of error as published. Only pairwise tests are used to
determine significance in the ranking tables; no multiple comparison methods are used.
12.4 Variance Replicate Tables
Beginning with the 2010–2014 5-year data, the ACS has published the Variance Replicate
Estimate Table package, which allows advanced users to calculate variances, standard errors, and
margins of error using replicate estimates, as described in section 12.2 above.
The table package provides augmented versions of over 100 detailed ACS tables published on
data.census.gov for a limited set of geographic summary levels. For this limited set of tables, the
standard errors and eighty variance replicate estimates are provided in addition to the original
published estimates and margins of error for each characteristic. This additional information
allows data users to compute variances using the replicate methodology similar to the one used
by the ACS during its production. The benefit of using this set of expanded tables is that it
allows advanced data users to calculate MOEs for their own defined characteristics without using
approximation formulas. This includes forming their own aggregate estimates across geographies
or aggregating categories within a table, as well as ratios, proportions, and percentages.
12.5 Variance Estimation for the PUMS
The Census Bureau cannot possibly predict all combinations of estimates and geography that
may interest data users. Data users can download PUMS files and tabulate the data to create their
own estimates. Because the ACS PUMS contains only a subset of the full ACS sample, estimates
from the ACS PUMS file will often differ from the published ACS estimates based on the full
ACS sample.
Users of the ACS PUMS files can compute the estimated variances of their statistics using one of
two options: (1) the replication method using replicate weights released with the PUMS data and

The design factor (DF) is the ratio of the standard error of an estimated parameter (computed

is computed as follows:
The value 39 in the formula above is the FPC factor based on an approximate sampling fraction
of 2.5 percent in the ACS; that is, (100 − 2.5) / 2.5 = 97.5 / 2.5 = 39.
The value of DF is obtained by fitting the no-intercept regression model RSE = DF × SRSSE
using standard errors (RSE, SRSSE) for various published table estimates at the national and state
levels. The values of DFs by topic can be obtained from the “PUMS Accuracy of the Data”

documentation also provides examples on how to use the design factors to compute standard
errors for the estimates of totals, means, medians, proportions or percentages, ratios, sums, and

The topics for the PUMS design factors are, for the most part, the same ones that were available

the estimate is a combination of two or more characteristics, the largest DF for this combination
of characteristics is used. The only exceptions to this are items crossed with race or Hispanic
origin; for these items, the largest DF is used after removing the race or Hispanic origin DFs
from consideration.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-1
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13.1 Overview
This chapter discusses the data products derived from the American Community Survey (ACS).
ACS data products include the tables, reports, and files that contain estimates of social,
economic, housing, and demographic characteristics. These products cover geographic areas
within the United States and Puerto Rico.
Most surveys of the population provide sufficient samples to support the release of data products
only for the nation, the states, and perhaps select sub-state geographic areas. The ACS is a very
large nationwide survey that collects data continuously every year and designed to provide data
products for many types of geographic areas, including smaller geographic areas such as places,
townships, and census tracts.
The first step in the preparation of a data product is defining the topics and characteristics it will
cover. Once the initial characteristics are determined, they must be reviewed by the Census
Bureau Disclosure Review Board (DRB) to ensure that individual responses and the identity of
individual respondents will be kept confidential. Based on this review, the specifications of the
products may be revised. The DRB also may require that the microdata files be altered in certain
ways and may impose restrictions on the publication of estimates for certain geographic areas
based on the size of the ACS sample or the population size of such areas. These activities are
collectively referred to as disclosure avoidance.
The actual processing of the data products cannot begin until all response records for a given
year or years are edited and imputed in the data preparation and processing phases, the final
weights are determined, and disclosure avoidance techniques are applied. Using the weights, the
sample data are tabulated for a wide variety of characteristics according to the predetermined
content. These tabulations are done for selected geographic areas for the 1-year products and for
small geographic areas down to the census tract and block group levels for 5-year products. The
Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS) data files are created by different processes because the
data are a subset of the full sample data.
After the estimates are produced, Census Bureau subject matter analysts review them for
reasonableness. When the estimates have passed the final review, they are released to the public.
PUMS data files are reviewed in a separate process.
While the 2005 ACS sample was limited to the housing unit (HU) population for the United
States and Puerto Rico, starting in sample year 2006, the ACS was expanded to include the group
quarters (GQ) population. This step made the 2006 ACS and Puerto Rico Community Survey
(PRCS), published in 2007, representative of the total resident population of the United States
and Puerto Rico.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-2
In 2008, the Census Bureau, for the first time, released products based on three years of ACS
sample data collected from 2005 through 2007 for areas of 20,000 people or greater. However,
the 3-year product was subsequently discontinued after the release of the 2011-2013 ACS 3-year
estimates. In 2010, the Census Bureau released the first products based on five years of
consecutive ACS samples, 2005 through 2009. Since several years of sample form the basis of
these multiyear products, reliable estimates can be released for much smaller geographic areas
than is possible for products based on single-year data. Starting with the 2014 data year, the
Census Bureau began releasing 1-year Supplemental Estimates, which are simplified versions of
popular ACS Detailed Tables for geographic areas with at least 20,000 people.
In addition to data products regularly released to the public, the Census Bureau releases other
data products developed through a fee-based custom tabulations program. Government agencies,
private organizations and businesses, or individual data users can request custom tabulations of
data. As is the case for regular data products, custom tabulation requests are reviewed and
approved by the DRB to assure protection of the confidentiality of individual responses. Section
13.7 provides information on methods used by the DRB to protect census data, including data
from the ACS.
Chapter 14 describes the dissemination of the data products discussed in this chapter, the ACS
data release schedule, and the locations of various products and supporting documents on the
Census Bureau’s website.
13.2 Geography
The Census Bureau strives to provide ACS estimates for the geographic areas that are most
important to data users. The two main types of geographic areas for which ACS estimates are
provided include legal areas and statistical areas.
Legal areas are defined specifically by law and include state, local, and tribal government units
(e.g., Alaska Native Regional Corporation [ANRC] areas, states, counties, minor civil divisions
[MCDs], and incorporated places), as well as specially defined administrative areas, such as
congressional districts and state legislative districts.
Statistical areas are defined to tabulate and provide census data for areas not specifically defined
by law. Some statistical areas are defined directly by the Census Bureau and state, regional, or
local authorities (e.g., census tracts, block groups, and census designated places [CDPs]), while
other statistical areas, such as Core Based Statistical Area (CBSA) geographies (e.g.,
Metropolitan Statistical Area, Micropolitan Statistical Area, Combined Statistical Area [CSA],
and Metropolitan Division), are defined and maintained by the Office of Management and
Budget (OMB).

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-3
“Geographic Summary Level” is a term used by the Census Bureau to designate the different
geographic levels or types of geographic areas for which data are summarized. Geographic
components describe subsets within summary levels and are most commonly used to identify
population ranges within a summary level. See Geography & ACS for more information about
the types of geographic areas for which the Census Bureau publishes ACS estimates and other
resources.
The ACS 1-year estimates are published annually for recognized legal, administrative, or
statistical areas with populations of 65,000 or more. The population sizes are based on the latest
ACS population estimates. If a geographic area met the 1-year threshold for the previous period
but dropped below it for the current period, data will continue to be published for that area if the
population does not drop more than five percent. The ACS 5-year estimates, based on five
successive years of ACS samples, are published for all legal, administrative, and statistical areas
down to the block group level, regardless of population size. The release of ACS estimates is
also subject to confidentiality restrictions and data quality filtering, which are covered in more
detail in Section 13.7.
The PRCS provides estimates for legal, administrative, and statistical areas in Puerto Rico. The
data release and threshold rules, confidentiality restrictions, and data quality filtering for the U.S.
resident population also apply to the publication of data for the PRCS.
Many areas for which ACS estimates are produced undergo periodic boundary changes because
of annexation, boundary correction, deannexation, consolidation with other areas, or
redistricting. Each year, the Census Bureau’s Geography Division, working with state and local
governments, conducts the Boundary and Annexation Survey (BAS) to collect updated boundary
information.62F
63 In addition to the BAS, there are a variety of update programs underway in the
Geography Division at any given time. In addition, the Redistricting Data Program periodically
collects state legislative and congressional district boundaries when they are changed by the
states through redistricting.63F
64
Twice a year, the Geography Division stops all update programs and performs an activity called
benchmarking. At this time a ‘snapshot’ is taken of the Master Address File (MAF)
Topologically Integrated Geographic and Encoding Reference (TIGER) database, or
MAF/TIGER database (MTdb). All of the address and geographic units used in the ACS sample
and data products come from these Geography Division biannual benchmarks conducted in
December and July.
63 See About BAS for more information.
64 See Redistricting Data Program for more information.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-4
In the case of multiyear estimates, the boundaries of the legal entity for which the estimate is
produced are those of the final year of data collection for that multiyear estimate. For example,
geographic units for which a 2016–2020 estimate is produced are those in effect in 2020.
13.3 Defining the Data Products
The initial set of data products for the ACS was developed by starting with the 2000 Census
Summary File tables and gathering information on public data needs through several Federal
Register Notices.
The ACS Data Products Planning Working Group (DPPWG) and ACS Portfolio Management
Governance Board (PMGB) have played a critical role in establishing more efficient processes
for considering, reviewing, and approving proposals for new and modified data products. These
groups have helped to allocate resources appropriately to develop new products, to consider
innovative product design approaches suggested by both internal and external groups, and to
revise existing products to make them more useful. They also help to ensure that technical
constraints are fully assessed against technical requirements and that potential product
innovations align with future goals. These groups also help develop data products when new
topics are introduced to the ACS or new topics using existing data are identified.
The ACS Data Users Group (DUG) was formed in early 2013 to provide regional planners,
demographers, community leaders, and other data users with an externally managed forum for
exchanging information on understanding and using ACS data. The group includes an online
community and helpful resources, such as links to ACS reference materials and webinars.
Membership in the group is free and is open to all interested ACS data users. Since 2013,
membership in the ACS Data Users Group has grown to over 5,000 members. More information
is available on ACS DUG activities at: https://acsdatacommunity.prb.org/.
In 2015, the ACS Data Product Redesign Group (DPRG) was established to provide the Census
Bureau with proactive feedback on the current suite of data products in terms of usage and gaps,
and to provide feedback on changes or revisions to data products and data dissemination
methods. The feedback from the DPRG facilitated the decision to release the ACS 1-year
Supplemental Estimates and the ACS 5-year Variance Replicate Tables. In 2021, the former
ACS Data Products Redesign Group (DPRG) merged with the ACS Data Users Group Steering
Committee to form one ACS Data Users Group Steering Committee.
In partnership with the U.S. Census Bureau and the Population Reference Bureau (PRB), the
ACS DUG is led by a steering committee representing a broad spectrum of ACS data users. The
DUG Steering Committee’s main responsibilities are improving data users’ understanding of the
value and utility of ACS data. In addition, the DUG Steering Committee informs PRB and
Census Bureau staff about the concerns of ACS data users and provides feedback on ACS-
related activities, data products, dissemination channels, and/or methodology. The DUG Steering

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-5
Committee members also help plan ACS Data Users Conferences with PRB and the Census
Bureau, which are held biennially to broaden understanding about the importance of the ACS.
The first ACS Data Users Conference was held in 2014.
13.4 Description of Tabular Data Products
The ACS releases new data every year through a variety of data tables and other datasets that are
accessible with different data tools. (See Chapter 14 for how we release these products to the
public.)
Data for the ACS are collected from a sample of housing units (HUs), as well as the GQ
population, and are used to produce estimates of the actual figures that would have been obtained
by interviewing the entire population. The data tables contain the estimates from the survey
responses. Each estimate is created using the sample weights from respondent records that meet
certain criteria. For example, the ACS estimate of people under the age of 18 in Chicago for a
given year is calculated by adding the weights from all respondent records from interviews
completed in that year in Chicago with residents under 18 years old.
This section provides a description of each tabular product. Each product described is available
as 1-year period estimates; unless otherwise indicated, they are also available as 5-year
estimates. The data products described below contain all estimates planned for release each year,
including those from multiple years of data. Data release rules described in Section 13.7 prevent
certain 1-year period estimates from being released if they do not meet ACS requirements for
statistical reliability. All ACS estimates within tabular data products are accompanied by a
margin of error at a 90 percent confidence level. Unless specifically mentioned, these ACS data
products are available at data.census.gov.
Detailed Tables
The Detailed Tables provide basic distributions of characteristics. They are the foundation upon
which other data products are built. They include social, economic, housing, and demographic
characteristics and provide 1- or 5-year period estimates for the nation and states, as well as for
counties, towns, and other small geographic entities such as census tracts and block groups, the
latter of which are only available as 5-year estimates.
The Census Bureau’s initial goal in defining ACS data products was to maintain a high degree of
comparability between ACS Detailed Tables and Census 2000 Summary File 3 sample-based
data products. In addition, characteristics not measured in the 2000 Census tables were included
in the ACS base tables. For example, the 2022 ACS data products included more than 1,400
Detailed Tables that cover a wide variety of social, economic, housing, and demographic
characteristics.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-6
Data Profiles
There are four Data Profiles, one each for social, economic, housing, and demographic
characteristics, and they contain a summary of statistics for each topic in the ACS. For a given
geographic area, the Data Profiles include distributions for such characteristics as sex, age, type
of household, race and Hispanic origin, school enrollment, educational attainment, disability
status, veteran status, language spoken at home, ancestry, income, poverty, physical housing
characteristics, occupancy and owner/renter status, and housing value. The profiles provide both
point estimates and percentage estimates of characteristics.
Comparison Profiles
Beginning with the 2007 ACS, the Census Bureau published a 1-year Comparison Profile that
compares the sample year’s estimates with those of each of the four previous years. For example,
the 2019 Comparison Profile compared 2019 ACS 1-year estimates with those of the 2015, 2016,
2017, and 2018 ACS 1-year estimates. These profile reports include the results of a statistical
significance test for each previous year’s estimate compared to the current year. This test result
indicates whether the previous year’s estimate is significantly different (at a 90 percent
confidence level) from the current year's. Beginning with the 2011–2015 ACS 5-year release, the
5-year Comparison Profiles show data side-by-side from two different non-overlapping 5-year
periods, indicating where there is a statistically significant difference between the two sets of
estimates. For example, the 2018–2022 Comparison Profile compares estimates from 2013–2017
with those from the 2011–2015 period.
Narrative Profiles
Sourced from the Data Profiles, the 5-year Narrative Profiles allow users to explore a narrative
and graphical presentation of the statistics for their own communities. This descriptive report
describes a geographic area using custom text and graphics across 18 topics. This product is
available using the 5-year data and can be generated for the Nation, States (including Puerto
Rico), Counties, County Subdivisions, Places, Metropolitan/Micropolitan Statistical Areas, ZIP
Code Tabulation Areas (ZCTAs), Census Tracts, and American Indian/Alaska Native /Hawaiian
Home Land (AIA/ANA/HHL) Areas.64F
65
Subject Tables
Subject Tables cover frequently requested information on a single topic for a single geographic
area. However, Subject Tables contain more detail than the ACS Data Profiles. In general, a
Subject Table contains distributions for a few key universes, such as the race groups and people
in various age groups, which are relevant to the topic of the table. The estimates for these
65 The 5-year Narrative Profiles are available through the ACS website starting with the 2011-2015 ACS data.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-7
universes are displayed as whole numbers. The distribution that follows is displayed in
percentages. For example, Subject Table S1501 on educational attainment provides the estimates
for two different age groups—18 to 24 years old and 25 years and older— as a whole number.
For each age group, these estimates are followed by the percentages of people in different
educational attainment categories (high school graduate, college undergraduate degree, etc.).
Subject Tables also contain other measures, such as medians, and they include the imputation
rates for relevant characteristics. About 70 topic-specific Subject Tables are released each year.
Selected Population Profiles
Selected Population Profiles (SPPs) provide certain characteristics from the Data Profiles for a
specific race, Hispanic origin, ancestry, and place of birth group. SPPs are provided every year
for 1-year estimates. There are two thresholds used for SPPs. First, the SPPs are released for
geographic areas with a total population of at least 500,000. Before the 2007 ACS, SPPs were
produced for geographic areas with a total population of at least 1,000,000. Second, we look at
the size of the population group of interest. The SPPs are released for population groups with an
estimated population of 65,000 or more within a given geographic area.
Selected Population Tables and American Indians and Alaska Native Tables
The ACS Selected Population Tables and American Indian and Alaska Native (AIAN) Tables
include a set of data products comparable to the Census 2000 Summary File 4/AIAN products.
Representing a collection of Detailed Tables and Data Profiles, this product, created using ACS
5-year estimates, provides data iterated by detailed race, ethnicity, tribal, and ancestry population
groups. This product was first released in May 2012 and included estimates for the period 2006
to 2010. The Census Bureau produces the Selected Population Tables and American Indian and
Alaska Native Tables approximately every five years.
Report Production Series and Analytical Applications
Subject matter analysts at the Census Bureau create analytical data products based on recent
ACS data. The analytical products include reports, infographics, and data visualizations. These
products provide data users with insightful analysis on the current state of the nation. Some
subject matter areas produce special tabulations of the data for their topics. The Census Bureau
also produces methodological analysis products based on ACS collection and processing
research.65F
66
13.5 Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS)
The PUMS files comprise individual records that contain information collected about each
person and housing unit (HU). PUMS files are extracts from the microdata for which disclosure
66 Information on these various analytic products can be found in the ACS Library website.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-8
avoidance techniques are applied to protect confidential information about households or
individuals. These extracts cover the same characteristics contained in the full microdata sample
files. The only geography other than region, division, and state shown on a PUMS file is the
Public Use Microdata Area (PUMA). PUMAs are special non-overlapping areas that partition a
state; each PUMA contains a population of about 100,000 or more. State governments draw the
PUMA boundaries based on the Census Bureau’s criteria and guidelines at the time of the
Census.66F
67 PUMAs are redefined after each decennial census, and the new PUMAs are introduced
into the ACS in years ending in “2,” so ACS PUMS records through 2021 have the 2010
PUMAs, and the ACS records from 2022 on have the 2020 PUMAs.
The Census Bureau releases 1-year and 5-year PUMS files. The multiyear PUMS files combine
annual PUMS files to create larger samples in each PUMA, covering a longer period of time.
The 5-year PUMS files include all of the records on the individual 1-year PUMS files, with items
like income and geography brought forward to the latest year. This allows users to create
estimates that are more statistically reliable.
13.6 Multiyear Estimates
While the types of data products for the multiyear estimates are almost entirely identical to those
used for the 1-year estimates, there are several distinctive features of the multiyear estimates that
data users must bear in mind.67F
68
First, the geographic boundaries that are used for multiyear estimates are based on the same set
of geographic boundaries as the single-year estimates from the last year in the estimate period.
For example, the 2018-2022 ACS 5-year estimates and 2022 ACS 1-year estimates were
tabulated on the same vintage of geographic boundaries. If a geographic area has gained or lost
territory over time, it can affect the user’s interpretation of estimates for that geographic area
when making comparisons over time. To learn about the specific vintage of geographies used in
a particular reference year of the ACS, please visit the Census Bureau’s Geography Boundaries
by Year website.
Secondly, for multiyear period estimates based on monetary characteristics (for example, median
earnings), inflation factors are applied to the data to create estimates that reflect the dollar values
in the final year of the multiyear period.
Finally, changes to the questionnaire may occur from time to time. Whenever possible, changes
are crosswalked forward to make the data comparable across time, as described in Chapter 10.
67 PDF-format maps of PUMA boundaries are available from the Census Bureau’s Geography Reference Maps
website.
68 References to multiyear data primarily refer to ACS 5-year estimates. ACS 3-year estimates were discontinued
after the release of the 2011-2013 ACS 3-year data.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-9
However, when crosswalking is not possible, changes to a question can result in the inability to
produce certain estimates for a multiyear period containing the year in which the question was
changed. In addition, if a new question is introduced, estimates of characteristics related to the
new question will not be available until they have been included for the entire multiyear period.
13.7 The Creation of Data Products
The subject matter analysts in the Census Bureau’s Social, Economic, and Housing Statistics
Division (SEHSD) and Population Division (POP) provide the specifications for ACS data
products. These specifications include the logic used to calculate every estimate in each data
product and the exact textual description associated with each estimate. The ACS Data Products
Planning Workgroup (DPPWG), which serves as a Change Control Board, approves
specification changes. After the review and approval of the table specifications by the DRB, the
specifications are validated and verified through a series of simulation and system tests. The
tabulation starts when the weighted and edited microdata become available (see Chapters 10 and
11). ACS data products are generated by various automated tabulation systems, beginning with
the 1-year period estimates for the Detailed Tables.
13.8 Data Release Rules
Various kinds of restrictions are applied to ACS data to limit the disclosure of information about
individual respondents and to limit the production of ACS estimates with unacceptable statistical
reliability.
Population Thresholds for ACS Data
Population thresholds restrict the availability of ACS 1-year estimates to populations of 65,000
or more. The Census Bureau started to release a subset of simplified Detailed Tables called the
ACS 1-year Supplemental Estimates with a population threshold of 20,000 or more beginning
with the 2014 ACS data. There is no population threshold for the 5-year estimates. Therefore,
areas or groups of all population sizes are eligible for 5-year estimates.68F
69
Other population threshold rules apply in cases where a drop in population size changes the
qualification of an area for types of ACS estimates for which it was previously qualified.
Consider, for example, the case of a geographic area that met the 1-year or 1-year Supplemental
Estimates population thresholds for a previous period but dropped below it for the current period.
The Census Bureau would like to preserve the continuity of data for such areas. To give such an
area a reasonable chance to retain access to 1-year or 1-year Supplemental Estimates, the Census
Bureau applies a data release rule that provides for the continued production of 1-year or 1-year
69 The Census Bureau produced 3-year estimates for populations of 20,000 or more from the 2005-2007 ACS 3-
year period through the 2011-2013 ACS 3-year period.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-10
Supplemental Estimates unless the population drops more than five percent below the specific
period threshold for 1-year or 1-year Supplemental Estimates.
Data Quality Filtering
Another kind of data release rule, data quality filtering, applies to ACS 1-year and 1-year
Supplemental Estimates. Every Detailed Table consists of a series of estimates. Each estimate is
subject to sampling variability that can be summarized by its standard error. If more than half of
the estimates in the table are not statistically different from zero (at a 90 percent confidence
level), then the table fails to meet the rule’s requirements and is not published. Dividing the
standard error by the estimate yields the coefficient of variation (CV) for each estimate. (If the
estimate is 0, a CV of 100 percent is assigned.) For each table at a given geographic area, CVs
are calculated for each table’s detailed estimates (excluding totals and subtotals), and the median
CV value is determined. If the median CV value for the table is less than or equal to 61 percent
(in other words, the median of the ratio of the margin of error to the estimate is less than one),
the table passes for that geographic area and is published; if it is greater than 61 percent, the table
fails and is not published.
Data quality filtering is not applied to 5-year estimates.
Disclosure Review Board Rules
Beyond the population thresholds and data quality filtering applied to ACS tables, the Disclosure
Review Board (DRB) establishes additional rules that protect against disclosing information
about individual respondents. These rules describe, for example, how medians or other quantiles
must be calculated, what requirements apply to medians, means, or aggregates, the thresholds for
tables involving specific kinds of geographic areas or tables with more than 100 data cells, and
thresholds for tables of unweighted counts of people and housing units.
The DRB uses data swapping as a disclosure limitation technique for the ACS. Data swapping
involves the swap, or interchange, of a small percentage of household records between pairs of
households in different geographic regions. The selection process for deciding which households
should be swapped is highly targeted to affect the records with the most disclosure risk. Pairs of
households that are swapped match on a minimal set of demographic variables, and the
household pairs, in most cases, are located in the same census tract. The methodology for
swapping is confidential to further protect the confidentiality of respondents. All data products
(tables and microdata) are created from the swapped data files.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-11
For PUMS data, the following techniques are employed in addition to swapping:
• Top-coding is a method of disclosure avoidance in which all cases in or above a certain
percentage of the distribution are placed into a single category.
• Geographic population thresholds prohibit the disclosure of data for individuals or HUs
for geographic units with population counts below a specified level.
• Age perturbation (modifying the age of household members) is required for large
households containing 10 people or more because of concerns about confidentiality.
• Detail for categorical variables is collapsed if the number of occurrences in each
category does not meet a specified national minimum threshold.
• Partial synthesis is used to protect the confidentiality of housing units and persons at
risk for disclosure based on certain characteristics.
13.9 Data Review and Acceptance
After the editing, imputation, data products generation, disclosure avoidance, and application of
the release rules are complete, subject matter analysts perform a final review of the ACS data
and estimates before release. This final data review and acceptance process helps to ensure that
there are no obvious errors or other data anomalies.
Each year, the subject matter analysts review the ACS estimates following a multistep review
process and provide clearance before estimates are released to the public. Because of the short
time available to review such a large amount of data, the program has developed two tools: the
Automated Review Tool (ART) and the Visual Automated Review Tool (VisART), in addition
to various supplementary single- and multiyear review tools, to facilitate the process. These tools
enable subject matter analysts to detect statistically significant differences in estimates from one
year to the next using several statistical tests. ART features predesigned reports and includes
functions allowing for ad hoc, user-defined queries for hundreds of single-year estimates for
national, state, county, and place geographies. VisART includes a scatterplot, mapping
functionality, and the ability to create custom analyst queries with multiple variables. This tool
has been used for the review of 5-year estimates as well as portions of the 1-year review.
13.10 Custom Data Products
The Census Bureau offers a wide variety of general-purpose data products from the ACS
designed to meet the needs of most data users. They contain predefined sets of data for standard
census geographic areas. For users whose data needs are not met by the general-purpose
products, the Census Bureau offers customized tabulations on a cost-reimbursable basis through
the ACS custom tabulation program. Custom tabulations are created by tabulating data from

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products
13-12
ACS edited and weighted data files. These projects vary in size, complexity, and cost, depending
on the needs of the sponsoring client.
In many cases, requests for ACS custom tabulations from federal agencies with statutory
requirements for information have led to innovative approaches to providing ACS data. For
example, the Census Bureau fulfilled a Department of Justice tabulation request for ACS
estimates to meet Section 203 requirements of the Voting Rights Act by providing modeled ACS
estimates.
Each custom tabulation request is reviewed in advance by the DRB to ensure that confidentiality
is protected. The requestor may be required to modify the original request to meet disclosure
avoidance requirements.69F
70
70 See American Community Survey Custom Tabulations for more detailed information.

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) –
Chapter 13: Preparation and Review of Data Products

ACS and PRCS Design and Methodology (Version 4.0) – Chapter 14: Data Dissemination
14-1
Chapter 14: Data Dissemination
14.1 Overview
This chapter provides a description of the release schedule and dissemination channels for the
American Community Survey (ACS) data products, including the 1-year estimates, 1-year
Supplemental Estimates, 5-year estimates, and Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS) files.
The primary data dissemination systems for ACS data products are:
• Standard ACS 1-year, 1-year Supplemental Estimates, and 5-year tabulated data
products are available using the Census Bureau’s main data dissemination
environment, data.census.gov, and using the Census Bureau’s Application
Programming Interface (API).
• The ACS Summary File for Detailed Tables, along with supporting documentation,
is available on the ACS Summary File webpage.
• ACS 1-year Geographic Comparison Tables, 1-year Ranking Tables, and 5-year
Narrative Profiles are available through the ACS data tables page.
• ACS PUMS files are available for download through the ACS PUMS webpage.
• In addition, the ACS Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS) are available for use
using the Microdata Analysis Tool (MDAT).
• ACS 5-year Variance Replicate Estimates (VRE) Tables are available for a subset
of Detailed Tables through the VRE Tables page.
14.2 Release Schedule
The Census Bureau typically releases the ACS 1-year estimates in September; ACS 1-year
PUMS files and ACS 1-year Supplemental Estimates in October; ACS 5-year estimates in
December; and ACS 5-year PUMS files and 5-year Variance Replicate Estimates in January of
the following year. Table 14-1 shows the general ACS data release schedule.

14.3 Accessing ACS Data and Supporting Documents
Data.census.gov
In 2019, data.census.gov was launched as the new platform and an integral part of
www.census.gov for accessing data and digital content from the U.S. Census Bureau. One of the
goals of the new platform was to streamline duplicative data access tools and capabilities by
incorporating the best aspects of each tool into a single platform. The 2018 ACS 1-year estimates
were the first ACS data release to be disseminated via data.census.gov and marked the end of
any releases into the former data dissemination platform, American FactFinder (AFF). AFF was
officially retired in March 2020. ACS data available on data.census.gov include all 1- and 5-year
releases since the 2010 1-year and 2006-2010 5-year. Older releases and the 3-year products are
available via FTP and the API.
Data.census.gov is the primary online tool for accessing ACS data products, which include
Detailed Tables, Data Profiles, Comparison Profiles, Subject Tables, 1-year Supplemental
Estimates, and Selected Population Profiles (1-year data only). In addition, microdata access
through MDAT allows access to the PUMS files.

Other ACS Data Tools
In addition to data.census.gov, the Census Bureau’s primary tool for accessing data from the
ACS, there are many other ways to access ACS estimates. Popular tools include:
• QuickFacts-Provides quick, easy access to facts about people, business, and
geography
• My Congressional District-Interactive tool that provides the latest demographic and
economic statistics for every Congressional District
• My Tribal Area-Interactive tool that provides the latest demographic and economic
statistics for tribal areas
• My Community Explorer-Interactive map-based tool that highlights demographic
and socioeconomic data that measures inequality
• OnTheMap for Emergency Management-Census Bureau’s data for disasters, natural
hazards, and weather events
• Census Business Builder-Interactive map that allows small business owners and
regional planners a way to easily navigate to and use key demographic and
economic data
TIGER70F
71 /Line Shapefiles with Selected Demographic and Economic Data-TIGER/Line
Shapefiles pre-joined with data in geodatabase format for mapping ACS data with Geographic
Information System (GIS) software. Visit the ACS Data Tools webpage for a complete list of our
data tools, along with additional information, to help you access the information you need from
the ACS.
ACS Website
The ACS website contains a wealth of information, documentation, and research papers about
the ACS program.71F
72 The site also hosts many supporting documents related to data products,
such as subject definitions, code lists, geographic information, comparison guidance, and the
latest information for each data release. Documentation on the accuracy of the data is also
included, providing information about the sample design, confidentiality, sampling error,
nonsampling error, and estimation methodology. Quality measures showing sample sizes,
coverage rates, response rates, and allocation rates are provided. The User and Errata Notes

section lists updates made to the data. The geography section gives a brief explanation of the
Census Bureau’s geographic hierarchy, common terms, and specific geographic areas presented.
The Guidance for Data Users section contains important information to help data users access
and use ACS data. The section includes information such as how to compare ACS data over
time, when to use 1-year or 5-year estimates, how to determine which ACS data table or tool to
use, and how to determine which ACS estimates are statistically different from one another. The
Guidance for Data Users section also contains a set of 14 ACS data user handbooks tailored to
specific data user audiences or tools. The handbooks include information on how to access and
use the data, including case studies featuring real-world applications of ACS data.
File Transfer Protocol (FTP) Site
The FTP site hosts the ACS Summary Files and PUMS files, and it is intended for advanced data
users.72F
73 The data and technical documentation can be downloaded into Excel, PDF, or text files.
Users of the FTP site can import the files into the spreadsheet/database software of their choice
for data analysis and table presentation. Documentation describing the layout of the site in the
README file is available in the main directory on the FTP server.
Application Programming Interface (API)
The Census Data Application Programming Interface (API) is a data service that enables
researchers and software developers to use Census Bureau data within their programs and
applications. The Census Data API presents data in a standardized way. Standardizing the API
query language across multiple datasets reduces the learning curve for researchers and
developers.
The API includes many Census Bureau datasets, including the ACS 1-year, 1-year Supplemental
Estimates, 3-year, and 5-year datasets.73F
74
Developers can build programs using statistical software, create mobile and Web apps, and
develop interactive data visualizations that display data directly in an app or browser window in
an easily consumable format. Examples of Census Bureau applications that draw ACS data
directly from the Census Data API include My Tribal Area and My Congressional District.74F
75
The Census Data API is also used widely by organizations outside of the Census Bureau. For
example, the Pennsylvania State Data Center uses the API to produce a series of dashboards

Federal Statistical Research Data Centers
The Census Bureau partners with various universities, research institutions, and agencies to form
a nationwide network of secure Federal Statistical Research Data Centers (FSRDC). At these
secure centers, qualified researchers with approved projects (i.e., projects that benefit Census
Bureau programs and demonstrate scientific merit) can perform statistical analysis on selected
internal microdata from the Census Bureau (including ACS) and other statistical agencies.77F
